nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/AI Agents and Tool Use
nodes.label: AI Agents and Tool Use
nodes.excerpt: AI agents extend LLMs from text generators to actors that can plan, call tools, observe results, and pursue goals over multiple steps. Tool use is implemented via function calling (structured JSON schema) or MCP. The agent loop is: reason → decide action → execute tool → observe result → continue. Key engineering concerns: prompt injection, tool permission scoping, parallelism, and graceful failure handling.

nodes.label: AI Alignment Overview
nodes.excerpt: AI alignment is the problem of ensuring AI systems pursue goals that match human intent — not just the proxy we specified. Current LLM alignment (RLHF, DPO, Constitutional AI) makes models helpful and less harmful. Future alignment research addresses whether very capable systems remain controllable, honest, and safe when humans can no longer easily evaluate their outputs.

nodes.label: AI Memory Architectures
nodes.excerpt: AI memory architectures define how agents store, retrieve, and use information across sessions. The core challenge is selecting the right memory at the right time — balancing in-context injection, vector retrieval, structured facts, and compression without drowning the model in noise or introducing privacy risk.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/AI Safety — Interpretability Research
nodes.label: AI Safety — Interpretability Research
nodes.excerpt: AI interpretability research reverse-engineers neural network internals to understand what models represent and why they behave as they do. Mechanistic interpretability (circuits, superposition, sparse autoencoders) is the dominant current approach, with Anthropic's work producing the first systematic maps of features inside large transformers.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Activation Functions (ReLU, GELU, Softmax)
nodes.label: Activation Functions (ReLU, GELU, Softmax)
nodes.excerpt: Activation functions are the nonlinear gates that make neural networks expressive. Without them, any depth of layers collapses to a single linear transformation. ReLU is the default for hidden layers in vision/MLP models. GELU and SwiGLU dominate transformers. Softmax converts logits to probabilities at output heads. Each has different gradient behavior, training stability, and dead-neuron risk.

nodes.label: Attention Mechanism — Self-Attention
nodes.excerpt: Self-attention computes a weighted mixture of value vectors for each token, where the weights come from comparing that token's query against every other token's key. It's the mechanism that lets transformers build context-aware representations and capture long-range dependencies — and why context windows are expensive.

nodes.label: Backpropagation Explained
nodes.excerpt: Backpropagation is the chain rule of calculus applied efficiently to a computation graph. Given a scalar loss, it computes ∂L/∂w for every weight w in the network in one backward pass — cost proportional to the forward pass, not the number of parameters. Modern frameworks (PyTorch, JAX) implement this as automatic differentiation over dynamic computation graphs.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization
nodes.label: Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization
nodes.excerpt: Normalization layers stabilize training by keeping activations in a reasonable range as gradients flow through many layers. BatchNorm normalizes across the batch dimension (works well in CNNs, breaks with small batches). LayerNorm normalizes across the feature dimension (standard in transformers, batch-size-independent). RMSNorm drops the mean-centering step and is the default in modern LLMs (Llama, Mistral).

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Benchmarks — MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, GPQA
nodes.label: Benchmarks — MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, GPQA
nodes.excerpt: AI benchmarks provide standardized capability comparisons across models. MMLU tests broad academic knowledge (57 subjects), HumanEval tests functional code generation, GSM8K tests multi-step math reasoning, GPQA tests expert-level science. Each has known limitations: contamination risk, saturation at the frontier, and poor correlation with actual application quality. Chatbot Arena's human preference rankings are more reliable for real-world usefulness.

nodes.label: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
nodes.excerpt: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting makes a model generate intermediate reasoning steps before producing a final answer. It works because reasoning tokens give the model additional compute budget to work through multi-step problems. CoT dramatically improves performance on math, logic, and planning tasks — but only in models large enough to actually reason (>7B parameters). Self-consistency (majority vote over multiple CoT samples) is the strongest zero-cost quality boost on top of CoT.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Chunking Strategies for RAG
nodes.label: Chunking Strategies for RAG
nodes.excerpt: Chunking splits documents into retrieval units. Chunk size and boundary choice are the highest-leverage variables in RAG quality — they determine retrieval precision, context completeness, and prompt budget. Fixed-size chunking is a baseline; structure-aware and semantic chunking dramatically improve results. Parent-child retrieval and contextual retrieval (Anthropic) are the current best practices.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Claude API and Anthropic SDK
nodes.label: Claude API and Anthropic SDK
nodes.excerpt: The Claude API uses a Messages interface where conversations are structured as alternating user/assistant turns with typed content blocks. Key practical concerns are model routing, prompt caching, extended thinking, tool use schemas, and token cost management.

nodes.label: Constitutional AI
nodes.excerpt: Constitutional AI (Anthropic, 2022) trains models using a written set of principles — a 'constitution' — rather than requiring human labels for every behavior. The model critiques and revises its own responses according to the principles (SL-CAI), then AI-generated preference comparisons train the final policy (RL-CAI / RLAIF). This scales better than RLHF for safety behaviors because the constitution can cover cases without per-example human annotation.

nodes.label: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
nodes.excerpt: CNNs exploit spatial structure in images through shared-weight convolutional filters that detect local patterns (edges, textures, shapes) across the image. Stacking conv layers builds hierarchical representations from pixels to objects. ResNet's skip connections solved deep network training; EfficientNet scaling optimizes across depth/width/resolution. Vision Transformers (ViT) now compete for accuracy at scale, but CNNs remain dominant in edge deployment and real-time detection.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Cosine Similarity and Distance Metrics
nodes.label: Cosine Similarity and Distance Metrics
nodes.excerpt: Distance metrics define what 'similar' means in vector space. Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors (direction, not magnitude) — the standard for text embeddings. Dot product combines direction and magnitude. Euclidean measures geometric distance. The right metric must match how the embedding model was trained; using the wrong one degrades retrieval quality. ANN indexes are metric-specific.

nodes.label: Diffusion Models
nodes.excerpt: Diffusion models generate images by learning to reverse a gradual noise-addition process. Training teaches the model to denoise at any noise level; inference starts from pure noise and iteratively denoises to a clean image. Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion) operates in a compressed latent space for efficiency. Classifier-free guidance (CFG) controls how strongly the output follows the text prompt.

nodes.label: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
nodes.excerpt: DPO eliminates the reward model and RL loop from RLHF by reparameterizing the reward directly in terms of the policy ratio. The result is a supervised binary cross-entropy loss on preference pairs that implicitly optimizes the same objective as RLHF. It's simpler, more stable, and now the default for open-source LLM alignment.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Emergent Abilities in Large Models
nodes.label: Emergent Abilities in Large Models
nodes.excerpt: Emergent abilities appear absent in smaller models and present in larger ones, but the distinction between genuine phase transitions and metric artifacts is actively debated. The Schaeffer et al. (2023) critique showed many apparent emergences vanish when evaluation metrics are changed — but inference-time scaling (chain-of-thought, o1/o3-style reasoning) represents a second scaling axis that produces genuinely new capability patterns.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning
nodes.label: Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning
nodes.excerpt: Zero-shot: the model performs a task from instructions alone with no examples. Few-shot: provide k examples in the prompt and the model generalizes the pattern. Both work through in-context learning — the model uses its pre-trained knowledge conditioned on your demonstration, without any weight updates. Scale is the prerequisite: small models barely generalize; large models generalize reliably.

nodes.label: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
nodes.excerpt: GANs pit a generator (creates fake samples) against a discriminator (distinguishes real from fake) in a minimax game. The adversarial training produces sharp, realistic samples — but training is notoriously unstable and prone to mode collapse. Diffusion models have largely replaced GANs for image generation, but GANs remain dominant for real-time applications, super-resolution, and video synthesis where diffusion's many inference steps are too slow.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Gradient Descent Variants (SGD, Adam, AdaGrad)
nodes.label: Gradient Descent Variants (SGD, Adam, AdaGrad)
nodes.excerpt: Gradient descent variants are optimizers that turn gradients into parameter updates. SGD with momentum is simple and generalizes well. Adam adapts per-parameter learning rates using running estimates of gradient magnitude — it's the default for most LLM training. AdamW fixes a weight decay bug in Adam. Learning rate scheduling (warmup + cosine decay) matters as much as the optimizer choice.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Hallucination — Causes and Mitigations
nodes.label: Hallucination — Causes and Mitigations
nodes.excerpt: Hallucination is when an LLM generates output that is false, fabricated, or unsupported by its context. It's not a bug to be patched — it's a predictable consequence of probabilistic next-token generation optimized for fluency. Mitigation is architectural: RAG, citations, tool use, constrained decoding, and grounding prompts reduce it; they don't eliminate it. High-stakes systems need external verification.

nodes.label: Knowledge Distillation
nodes.excerpt: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to match a larger teacher's outputs (soft probability distributions) rather than just hard ground-truth labels. Soft labels carry information about class similarity and uncertainty that hard labels discard. For LLMs, distillation means training a small model on teacher-generated text. DistilBERT: 40% smaller, 97% BERT performance, 60% faster. DeepSeek R1-Distill used this to transfer reasoning ability to 7B-70B models cheaply.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Local LLMs (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio)
nodes.label: Local LLMs (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio)
nodes.excerpt: Local LLMs run open-weight models on personal hardware using tools like Ollama, llama.cpp, and LM Studio. GGUF quantization makes large models fit in consumer RAM, but performance depends heavily on hardware — a 70B model at Q4 needs ~40GB and delivers ~10–20 tokens/sec on a modern GPU.

nodes.label: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
nodes.excerpt: LSTMs add explicit memory management to RNNs via three gates (forget, input, output) and a separate cell state pathway. The cell state provides a gradient highway through time, enabling learning over 100+ step dependencies where plain RNNs fail. GRU is a simpler 2-gate variant that's often equally effective. Both are now largely superseded by transformers for NLP but remain relevant for time series, embedded applications, and streaming contexts.

nodes.label: Loss Functions
nodes.excerpt: The loss function defines what a model is optimizing for. Cross-entropy is the standard for classification and language modeling. MSE for regression. Contrastive losses for embedding/representation learning. The choice encodes your real objective — a mismatch between loss and what you actually care about (e.g., optimizing accuracy proxy instead of business metric) will faithfully produce a model that's good at the wrong thing.

nodes.label: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
nodes.excerpt: MoE replaces dense FFN layers in transformers with N expert FFNs, routing each token to only k of them (typically k=1 or 2). Total parameters scale with N×expert_size, but compute per token scales with k×expert_size — you get a much larger model for similar inference cost. Mixtral 8x7B has 8 experts, activates 2 per token: 47B total params but ~13B active — quality like a 30B dense model at 13B inference cost.

nodes.label: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
nodes.excerpt: MCP is Anthropic's open protocol for connecting AI applications to external tools, data sources, and context providers via a standardized JSON-RPC interface. It separates capability providers (MCP servers) from model applications (MCP hosts), letting a single server be reused across many AI tools.

nodes.label: Model Evaluation Metrics
nodes.excerpt: Evaluation metrics measure what the model is actually doing, not what the training loss measured. Classification uses precision/recall/F1/AUC. Generation uses perplexity, BLEU/ROUGE (poor for quality), human evaluation, and LLM-as-judge. For LLM applications: RAGAS for RAG quality, MT-Bench for instruction following, Chatbot Arena for overall preference. The right metric must match the actual user goal.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Model Quantization (INT4, INT8, GGUF)
nodes.label: Model Quantization (INT4, INT8, GGUF)
nodes.excerpt: Quantization reduces model weights from float16/32 to int8 or int4, cutting memory 2–4× with manageable quality loss. Q8 is nearly lossless; Q4 is the practical sweet spot for local inference; Q3 and below start causing noticeable degradation. GGUF is the file format for llama.cpp-based local inference. GPTQ and AWQ are GPU-optimized post-training quantization formats. Knowing the memory math tells you what model fits on your hardware.

nodes.label: Multi-Head Attention
nodes.excerpt: Multi-head attention runs h independent self-attention operations in parallel, each on a lower-dimensional subspace. The heads can specialize in different relationship types. Their outputs are concatenated and projected back to d_model. GQA and MQA reduce the KV cache memory cost by sharing key/value heads across query heads.

nodes.label: Multimodal Models
nodes.excerpt: Multimodal models process and reason across multiple data types — text, images, audio, video. The core architecture challenge is aligning different modality representations into a shared space where a single model can reason across them. GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet are the leading multimodal LLMs; Whisper handles speech; Stable Diffusion handles image generation. Real-time voice (speech-in, speech-out) is the frontier as of 2025.

nodes.label: Neural Network Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: A neural network is a function approximator built from stacked linear transformations and nonlinearities. Each layer computes Wx + b, then applies an activation function. Depth (many layers) enables hierarchical feature learning. Training adjusts weights via backpropagation + gradient descent to minimize a loss. Understanding the fundamentals explains why networks work, why they fail, and what every more-advanced technique is built on top of.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/OpenAI API and GPT Models
nodes.label: OpenAI API and GPT Models
nodes.excerpt: The OpenAI API provides access to GPT, o-series reasoning, embedding, audio, and image models via a chat completions and responses interface. Key decisions involve model routing (o-series vs GPT-4o), tool/function calling schema, structured outputs, and the newer Assistants/Responses API vs the classic Chat Completions endpoint.

nodes.label: Positional Encoding
nodes.excerpt: Transformers are permutation-invariant — without positional encoding, 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' produce identical representations. Positional encodings inject order information. The field has evolved from fixed sinusoidal encodings → learned absolute positions → relative methods → RoPE, which is now the dominant approach in LLMs and enables better context length generalization.

nodes.label: Pre-training vs Fine-tuning
nodes.excerpt: Pre-training builds broad statistical representations from large-scale self-supervised learning. Fine-tuning adapts those representations to specific tasks, behaviors, or domains using smaller curated datasets. The practical decision — prompt vs RAG vs fine-tune — depends on what you're actually trying to change: knowledge, behavior, format, or capability. Fine-tuning changes weights; prompting and RAG don't.

nodes.label: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Prompt engineering is the craft of structuring model inputs to reliably get useful outputs. It's less about magic phrasing and more about clear task specification, appropriate context, and testing across input variations. For production systems: prompt + few-shot + output format spec + validation is the full stack. Fine-tuning is the escalation path when prompting doesn't reliably generalize.

nodes.label: RLHF vs RLAIF
nodes.excerpt: RLHF uses human preference labels to train aligned behavior; RLAIF replaces human labelers with AI-generated feedback, enabling 10–100x more data at lower cost. The risk is that RLAIF can amplify the feedback model's biases and blind spots — making the alignment only as good as the AI doing the evaluating.

nodes.label: ReAct Prompting Pattern
nodes.excerpt: ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) interleaves explicit reasoning steps (Thought:) with tool actions (Action:) and observations (Observation:) in a loop. The original prompting pattern preceded native function calling APIs. Today it's mostly implemented under the hood by agent frameworks, but the mental model — reason about what's missing, retrieve or act, update plan — is the foundation for all agentic LLM workflows.

nodes.label: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
nodes.excerpt: RNNs process sequences by maintaining a hidden state that's updated at each step. They were the dominant architecture for NLP and speech (2010-2017) before transformers replaced them. Vanishing gradients over long sequences are the core structural weakness — LSTMs and GRUs partially addressed it; transformers eliminated it entirely by replacing recurrence with attention.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Regularization (L1, L2, Dropout, Early Stopping)
nodes.label: Regularization (L1, L2, Dropout, Early Stopping)
nodes.excerpt: Regularization reduces overfitting by adding constraints or noise to training. L2 (weight decay) shrinks weights toward zero. L1 produces sparse weights. Dropout randomly disables neurons, acting as an ensemble of many smaller networks. Early stopping uses the validation curve as a signal. In modern LLM training, weight decay (AdamW) and dropout are the primary tools.

nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Reinforcement learning trains an agent to maximize cumulative reward through trial-and-error interaction with an environment. The core abstraction is the MDP (states, actions, transitions, rewards, discount). Q-learning learns action values; policy gradient methods directly optimize the policy. RL underpins game-playing AI, robotics, and the RLHF alignment pipeline for LLMs.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
nodes.excerpt: RLHF is a three-phase pipeline: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on demonstrations → reward model training on human preference comparisons → PPO policy optimization against the reward model with a KL constraint. It transformed raw pre-trained LLMs into useful assistants. Its costs — labeler disagreement, reward hacking, PPO instability — motivated DPO as a simpler alternative.

nodes.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
nodes.excerpt: RAG grounds LLM generation in retrieved external documents, enabling current, private, or large-scale knowledge without retraining. The pipeline has two phases: offline indexing (chunk → embed → store) and online retrieval (embed query → search → rerank → generate). Retrieval quality is almost always the bottleneck — not the LLM. Evaluating RAG means measuring retrieval and generation separately.

nodes.label: Scaling Laws
nodes.excerpt: Scaling laws are empirical power laws showing that model loss decreases predictably with more parameters, data, and compute. The Chinchilla finding (2022) showed that most models were undertrained — the optimal ratio is ~20 training tokens per parameter. Inference-time scaling (chain-of-thought, o1/o3 reasoning) is a second scaling axis discovered more recently.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Semantic Search vs Keyword Search
nodes.label: Semantic Search vs Keyword Search
nodes.excerpt: Keyword search matches exact terms with scoring algorithms like BM25. Semantic search embeds query and documents into vector space and retrieves by meaning. They fail in opposite directions: keyword misses paraphrases, semantic misses exact identifiers. Hybrid search (BM25 + dense retrieval + RRF fusion) almost always outperforms either alone and is the correct default for production RAG and search systems.

nodes.label: Speech Recognition (Whisper, Wav2Vec)
nodes.excerpt: Modern ASR converts audio to text using neural sequence models. Whisper (OpenAI) is an encoder-decoder transformer trained on 680K hours with multitask objectives — robust, multilingual, free. Wav2Vec 2.0 (Meta) uses self-supervised pre-training on unlabeled audio to learn speech representations that work with few labeled examples. Word Error Rate (WER) is the standard metric; Whisper large-v3 achieves <5% WER on clean English.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Text-to-Speech Models (ElevenLabs, Kokoro, Tortoise)
nodes.label: Text-to-Speech Models (ElevenLabs, Kokoro, Tortoise)
nodes.excerpt: TTS converts text to natural-sounding speech using a pipeline of text normalization → phoneme/duration prediction → mel spectrogram generation → neural vocoder. Modern neural TTS (ElevenLabs, Kokoro, VITS) produces near-human quality with emotional expressiveness. Voice cloning requires 5-30 seconds of reference audio. Streaming latency (time-to-first-audio) is the production constraint for conversational use — target <300ms.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
nodes.label: Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
nodes.excerpt: Tokenization splits text into subword units that a model embeds as vectors. BPE merges frequent character pairs iteratively; WordPiece maximizes language model likelihood; SentencePiece works on raw bytes and handles any language. Tokenization choices affect context window efficiency, multilingual quality, code handling, and numeric reasoning — and different models tokenize the same text differently.

nodes.label: Transfer Learning
nodes.excerpt: Transfer learning reuses representations learned on a source task to accelerate or improve learning on a related target task. In deep learning, early layers learn general features (edges, syntax) while later layers learn task-specific features — freeze the general layers, retrain the specific ones. The modern extreme: all LLM applications are transfer learning from a foundation model.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Transformer Architecture Deep Dive
nodes.label: Transformer Architecture Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: The transformer is a stack of attention + feed-forward blocks that processes tokens in parallel. It replaced RNNs as the dominant sequence architecture because attention captures long-range dependencies without vanishing gradients and scales efficiently on GPU hardware. Modern LLMs, vision transformers, and multimodal models are all transformer variants.

nodes.label: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)
nodes.excerpt: VAEs learn a smooth, structured latent space by encoding inputs to Gaussian distributions and regularizing with KL divergence. The reparameterization trick enables gradient flow through the sampling operation. VAEs produce blurrier samples than GANs or diffusion models, but their structured latent space enables interpolation and semantic editing. The latent diffusion model (Stable Diffusion) uses a VAE to compress images 48× before applying diffusion.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Vector Databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, ChromaDB, pgvector)
nodes.label: Vector Databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, ChromaDB, pgvector)
nodes.excerpt: Vector databases store high-dimensional embeddings and support fast approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search with metadata filtering. HNSW and IVF are the dominant index structures. The choice between dedicated vector DBs (Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate) and extensions (pgvector) depends on scale, operational complexity, and whether vectors live near relational data. For most applications under ~1M vectors, pgvector is sufficient.

nodes.id: Resources/AI & Machine Learning/Vector Embeddings — How They Work
nodes.label: Vector Embeddings — How They Work
nodes.excerpt: Vector embeddings map items (text, images, code, users) into dense numerical vectors where geometric proximity encodes semantic similarity. Embedding models are trained contrastively — push similar pairs together, pull dissimilar pairs apart. Dimension size, training data, and task-specific fine-tuning determine quality. OpenAI ada-002/3, Cohere, BGE, and E5 are the common choices for text.

nodes.label: Vision-Language Models
nodes.excerpt: VLMs connect a vision encoder (ViT/CLIP/SigLIP) to a language model via a projection layer. Images become 'visual tokens' the LLM can attend to alongside text. The vision encoder was originally CLIP (contrastively trained on image-text pairs); modern VLMs use SigLIP or DINOv2. Each image consumes 256-4096 tokens depending on resolution, explaining why vision is expensive. Key failure modes: counting, spatial reasoning, fine text.

nodes.label: AI Workflow
nodes.excerpt: multi-model AI workflow: Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, IDE Copilot routing guide.

nodes.label: API Design
nodes.excerpt: REST/RESTful, HTTP semantics, idempotency keys, pagination (cursor vs offset), versioning, auth (JWT/OAuth2), webhooks, REST vs GraphQL/gRPC/WebSockets/SSE/SOAP.

nodes.label: Agent Evaluation
nodes.excerpt: capability benchmarks (SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, OSWorld), safety eval (HarmBench, jailbreak taxonomy, dangerous capability evals), deployment monitoring, A/B testing, evaluation flywheel.

nodes.label: Agent Memory
nodes.excerpt: four memory types (working/episodic/semantic/procedural), Generative Agents retrieval formula, MemGPT, multi-agent memory consistency.

nodes.label: Agent Security
nodes.excerpt: security model for tool-using AI agents: prompt injection, capability-based tools, least privilege, sandboxing, memory poisoning, policy engines, audit logs, and Bobby permission modes.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Black Holes — Formation, Types, Hawking Radiation
nodes.label: Black Holes — Formation, Types, Hawking Radiation
nodes.excerpt: Black holes are regions where gravity is strong enough that nothing inside the event horizon can escape. They form through stellar collapse, mergers, and galactic growth, and theoretical Hawking radiation links gravity, quantum mechanics, and thermodynamics.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Cosmic Calendar (Carl Sagan) — Scale of the Universe
nodes.label: Cosmic Calendar (Carl Sagan) — Scale of the Universe
nodes.excerpt: The Cosmic Calendar compresses the universe's history into one calendar year, making deep time easier to grasp. On this scale, human civilization appears in the final moments of December 31.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Exoplanet Detection Methods (Transit, Radial Velocity)
nodes.label: Exoplanet Detection Methods (Transit, Radial Velocity)
nodes.excerpt: Exoplanets are usually detected indirectly through methods such as transits, radial velocity, direct imaging, microlensing, and astrometry. Each method has biases that shape what kinds of planets are easiest to find.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Gravitational Waves — LIGO and Discovery
nodes.label: Gravitational Waves — LIGO and Discovery
nodes.excerpt: Gravitational waves are ripples in spacetime produced by accelerating massive objects, especially compact-object mergers. LIGO's first direct detection opened a new observational channel for astronomy.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/James Webb Space Telescope — Key Discoveries
nodes.label: James Webb Space Telescope — Key Discoveries
nodes.excerpt: The James Webb Space Telescope observes infrared light to study early galaxies, star formation, exoplanet atmospheres, disks, nebulae, and solar-system objects. Its discoveries are reshaping details of cosmic history rather than replacing all prior astronomy.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Mars Colonization — Technical and Biological Challenges
nodes.label: Mars Colonization — Technical and Biological Challenges
nodes.excerpt: Mars colonization is constrained by radiation, low gravity, thin atmosphere, dust, cold, closed-loop life support, entry and landing, psychology, medicine, and supply chains. It is much harder than simply reaching Mars.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Orbital Mechanics Basics (Kepler, Delta-V, Hohmann Transfer)
nodes.label: Orbital Mechanics Basics (Kepler, Delta-V, Hohmann Transfer)
nodes.excerpt: Orbital mechanics describes motion under gravity using concepts such as Kepler's laws, energy, angular momentum, delta-v, and transfer orbits. Getting somewhere in space means changing orbit, not steering through a flat map.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Rocket Science Basics (Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation)
nodes.label: Rocket Science Basics (Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation)
nodes.excerpt: Rocket science is dominated by the rocket equation, which links velocity change to exhaust velocity and mass ratio. The equation explains why staging, high-efficiency engines, and low dry mass matter so much.

nodes.label: Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI)
nodes.excerpt: SETI searches for evidence of extraterrestrial technology, usually through radio, optical, infrared, and technosignature surveys. It is constrained by distance, assumptions about communication, signal rarity, and limited search coverage.

nodes.label: Solar System Overview
nodes.excerpt: The solar system includes the Sun, planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, comets, Kuiper Belt objects, and the distant Oort Cloud. Its structure reflects gravity, angular momentum, accretion, and billions of years of collisions and migration.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Space Exploration Timeline (Sputnik to Artemis)
nodes.label: Space Exploration Timeline (Sputnik to Artemis)
nodes.excerpt: Space exploration progressed from Sputnik and early human spaceflight through Apollo, space stations, robotic probes, reusable rockets, commercial launch, and Artemis. The timeline is a mix of science, geopolitics, engineering, and institutional risk.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/Stellar Evolution (Star Birth, Main Sequence, Supernovae, Remnants)
nodes.label: Stellar Evolution (Star Birth, Main Sequence, Supernovae, Remnants)
nodes.excerpt: Stellar evolution describes how stars form from gas clouds, spend most of their lives fusing hydrogen, and end as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes depending on mass. A star's mass is the dominant variable.

nodes.label: The Drake Equation
nodes.excerpt: The Drake Equation organizes the question of detectable extraterrestrial civilizations into factors such as star formation, planets, habitability, life, intelligence, technology, and civilization lifetime. Its value is conceptual more than predictive.

nodes.id: Resources/Astronomy & Space/The Fermi Paradox — Great Filter and Solutions
nodes.label: The Fermi Paradox — Great Filter and Solutions
nodes.excerpt: The Fermi Paradox asks why we see no clear evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations if the galaxy has many stars, planets, and time. Proposed answers include rarity, self-destruction, communication limits, non-expansion, and observation bias.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Aging Biology — Hallmarks of Aging
nodes.label: Aging Biology — Hallmarks of Aging
nodes.excerpt: The hallmarks of aging are biological processes associated with age-related decline, including genomic instability, telomere attrition, epigenetic changes, proteostasis loss, mitochondrial dysfunction, senescence, and altered signaling. The framework is useful but not a menu of proven human interventions.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/CRISPR — How Gene Editing Works
nodes.label: CRISPR — How Gene Editing Works
nodes.excerpt: CRISPR gene editing uses guide RNA and Cas enzymes to target specific DNA sequences for cutting or modification. It is powerful for research and therapy, but delivery, off-target effects, ethics, and long-term monitoring matter.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Cell Biology Basics (Organelles and Their Roles)
nodes.label: Cell Biology Basics (Organelles and Their Roles)
nodes.excerpt: Cell biology studies the structures and processes that make cells function. Organelles like the nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, and membranes divide cellular labor.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Epigenetics — Gene Expression Without Sequence Change
nodes.label: Epigenetics — Gene Expression Without Sequence Change
nodes.excerpt: Epigenetics studies changes in gene expression that do not alter DNA sequence, including DNA methylation, histone modification, and chromatin structure. It explains how context can affect gene activity without rewriting the genome.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Evolution — Natural Selection and Genetic Drift
nodes.label: Evolution — Natural Selection and Genetic Drift
nodes.excerpt: Evolution is change in heritable traits across generations, driven by mechanisms including natural selection, genetic drift, mutation, gene flow, and sexual selection. Selection is not intentional; it filters variation through differential survival and reproduction.

nodes.label: Evolutionary Psychology Overview
nodes.excerpt: Evolutionary psychology studies how evolved pressures may have shaped cognition, emotion, and behavior. It can generate useful hypotheses, but it is vulnerable to just-so stories when evidence is weak.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Genetics — DNA to Protein to Phenotype
nodes.label: Genetics — DNA to Protein to Phenotype
nodes.excerpt: Genetics links DNA sequence, gene expression, proteins, traits, and environment. Phenotype is not a simple one-gene output; it emerges from gene networks, regulation, development, and context.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Hormones Overview (Cortisol, Testosterone, Estrogen, Insulin, GH)
nodes.label: Hormones Overview (Cortisol, Testosterone, Estrogen, Insulin, GH)
nodes.excerpt: Hormones are signaling molecules that coordinate metabolism, stress, reproduction, growth, sleep, and energy balance. Cortisol, testosterone, estrogen, insulin, and growth hormone are context-dependent regulators, not simple good-or-bad chemicals.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Immune System Overview (Innate vs Adaptive)
nodes.label: Immune System Overview (Innate vs Adaptive)
nodes.excerpt: The immune system protects the body through innate fast defenses and adaptive targeted responses. Innate immunity recognizes broad patterns, while adaptive immunity uses B cells, T cells, antibodies, and memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Inflammation — Acute vs Chronic
nodes.label: Inflammation — Acute vs Chronic
nodes.excerpt: Acute inflammation is a short-term protective response to injury or infection, while chronic inflammation persists and can contribute to disease. The goal is regulation and resolution, not eliminating inflammation entirely.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Insulin and Blood Sugar Regulation
nodes.label: Insulin and Blood Sugar Regulation
nodes.excerpt: Insulin helps regulate blood glucose by signaling cells to take up and store nutrients. Blood sugar regulation depends on diet, activity, muscle, liver function, sleep, stress, hormones, and insulin sensitivity.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Longevity Research Overview (Sirtuins, mTOR, Senolytics)
nodes.label: Longevity Research Overview (Sirtuins, mTOR, Senolytics)
nodes.excerpt: Longevity research studies pathways and interventions that might affect healthspan or lifespan, including sirtuins, mTOR, senolytics, caloric restriction, exercise, and metabolic signals. Human evidence is much weaker than animal and mechanistic hype.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Mitochondria and ATP — The Energy Currency
nodes.label: Mitochondria and ATP — The Energy Currency
nodes.excerpt: Mitochondria are cellular organelles that convert energy from nutrients into ATP through pathways such as the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain. They also influence signaling, cell death, heat production, and metabolic health.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/Neurotransmitter Synthesis and Regulation
nodes.label: Neurotransmitter Synthesis and Regulation
nodes.excerpt: Neurotransmitters are synthesized, stored, released, detected, cleared, and recycled through tightly regulated processes. Their effects depend on receptors, brain regions, timing, transporters, enzymes, and circuit context.

nodes.label: Systems Biology
nodes.excerpt: Systems biology studies living organisms as interacting networks rather than isolated parts. It uses models, omics data, feedback loops, and pathway analysis to understand emergent biological behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/The Microbiome — Gut Flora and Health
nodes.label: The Microbiome — Gut Flora and Health
nodes.excerpt: The microbiome is the community of bacteria, archaea, fungi, viruses, and other organisms living in and on the body, especially in the gut. It influences digestion, immunity, metabolism, and signaling, but consumer claims often outrun evidence.

nodes.id: Resources/Biology & Life Sciences/The Stress Response (Fight-or-Flight, Freeze, Fawn)
nodes.label: The Stress Response (Fight-or-Flight, Freeze, Fawn)
nodes.excerpt: The stress response coordinates nervous-system, hormonal, and behavioral patterns such as fight, flight, freeze, and fawn. These responses can be adaptive in threat but costly when chronically activated.

nodes.label: Bobby Commands
nodes.excerpt: cheat sheet of all working Bobby voice commands and known gaps.

nodes.label: Bobby Memory Architecture
nodes.excerpt: ChromaDB + SQLite + Obsidian vault three-layer memory system.

nodes.id: Resources/Bobby/Bobby's ChromaDB Upgrade Path (Vector RAG at 300+ Notes)
nodes.label: Bobby's ChromaDB Upgrade Path (Vector RAG at 300+ Notes)
nodes.excerpt: Bobby currently uses `VAULT_INDEX.md` for vault routing, but the documented upgrade trigger is around 300 notes or when routing quality degrades. The upgrade path is to embed vault notes, store vectors locally in ChromaDB or pgvector, and use hybrid index-plus-vector retrieval.

nodes.label: Bobby's Config Reference (config.yaml Schema)
nodes.excerpt: Bobby should have a documented config schema for API keys, model routing, audio devices, wake word settings, memory paths, vault access, tool permissions, and phone bridge behavior. The exact current `config.yaml` schema is not captured in the vault yet, so this note is the intended reference shape plus open verification needs.

nodes.label: Bobby's Dangerous Command Rules
nodes.excerpt: Bobby needs strict rules for dangerous commands because it can control the OS, launch apps, access files, and eventually write to calendars or other services. Destructive, irreversible, privileged, external-facing, or privacy-sensitive actions should require confirmation and be logged.

nodes.label: Bobby's Known Bugs and Workarounds
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's known issues include ChromaDB context caps, WSL2 portproxy/IP drift, audio capture quirks, incomplete calendar/email/weather/messaging integrations, and model-routing uncertainty. This note gathers operational risks so debugging starts from known weak points.

nodes.label: Bobby's Memory System Design
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's memory design has three layers: ChromaDB for semantic memories, SQLite for structured history and preferences, and the Obsidian vault for durable high-signal knowledge. The design goal is fast recall for lightweight facts and richer retrieval for notes Bobby can reason over.

nodes.id: Resources/Bobby/Bobby's Model Routing Logic (Haiku vs Sonnet)
nodes.label: Bobby's Model Routing Logic (Haiku vs Sonnet)
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's current model plan is Haiku for fast routine interactions and Sonnet for complex reasoning, architecture, debugging, or multi-step synthesis. Routing should be based on task risk, ambiguity, context size, tool use, and expected reasoning depth.

nodes.label: Bobby's Obsidian Integration Design
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's Obsidian integration uses the vault as Layer 3 memory: scan `VAULT_INDEX.md`, choose relevant notes, fetch full content through the Obsidian Local REST API, and synthesize answers. The integration depends on disciplined index maintenance and WSL2-to-Windows portproxy routing.

nodes.label: Bobby's Personality and Tone Guide
nodes.excerpt: Bobby should feel like Kenny's local AI partner: direct, context-aware, useful, technically competent, and lightly personal without becoming performative. The tone target is pragmatic help with memory, initiative, and respect for Kenny's actual workflow.

nodes.label: Bobby's Phone App Guide (React PWA)
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's phone bridge is a React PWA served through Cloudflare Tunnel, giving mobile access to the local assistant backend. It should be treated as a remote-control surface with authentication, limited actions, clear connection state, and conservative tool permissions.

nodes.label: Bobby's Security Model
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's security model should treat the assistant as a local agent with access to voice, screen, vault notes, OS automation, API keys, and a phone bridge. The safe design is least privilege, explicit confirmation for risky actions, audit logs, local-first storage, and careful boundaries around remote access.

nodes.label: Bobby's Shortcut Templates
nodes.excerpt: Bobby shortcut templates are named command bundles such as `open the usual`, stored in SQLite and used to automate repeated app-launch workflows. Good shortcut design needs fuzzy matching, edit commands, confirmation for broad actions, and human-readable definitions Kenny can inspect.

nodes.label: Bobby's Test Coverage Map
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's test coverage should map the assistant's highest-risk paths: wake word, audio capture, STT, model routing, tool calls, memory writes, vault retrieval, OS automation, phone bridge, and safety confirmations. The vault does not yet document actual test coverage, so this note captures the coverage map to verify against the repo.

nodes.label: Bobby's Tool Registry — Full Reference
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's tool registry should describe every callable capability, what it can do, what inputs it accepts, what safety checks apply, and which layer owns it. The current working capabilities include app control, screenshots, screen understanding, memory lookup/write, Spotify playback, timers, vault retrieval, and phone bridge access.

nodes.label: Bobby's Upgrade Roadmap
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's roadmap after Phase 11B points toward proactive suggestions, calendar integration, stronger vault retrieval, memory compression, audit flags for Bobby-created notes, and clearer model routing. The roadmap should prioritize reliability and trust before more autonomy.

nodes.label: Bobby's Vault Index Strategy
nodes.excerpt: `VAULT_INDEX.md` is Bobby's current navigation layer: one concise routing sentence per note, kept under the context budget, updated whenever notes change, and used before fetching full content through Obsidian. Its quality determines whether Bobby finds the right vault knowledge.

nodes.label: Bobby's Voice Pipeline Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's voice pipeline starts with Porcupine wake-word detection, captures speech for Whisper transcription, routes text through the FastAPI backend and Claude, then streams spoken output through ElevenLabs. The WSL2 audio layer is a recurring constraint, especially for microphone capture and playback.

nodes.label: Bobby's WSL2 Quirks and Workarounds
nodes.excerpt: Bobby runs in WSL2, so recurring issues include audio capture, Windows host IP discovery, Obsidian REST API portproxy, localhost confusion, filesystem performance, and WSL restarts. These quirks should be treated as first-class operational knowledge rather than one-off setup trivia.

nodes.id: Resources/Bobby/Bobby's Wake Word and Audio Pipeline
nodes.label: Bobby's Wake Word and Audio Pipeline
nodes.excerpt: Bobby's wake word and audio path starts with Porcupine detecting `hey bobby`, then captures speech for Whisper and plays ElevenLabs output. In WSL2, audio reliability depends on PulseAudio/WSLg behavior, device permissions, sample rates, and robust fallback diagnostics.

nodes.label: Antifragile — Key Ideas (Taleb)
nodes.excerpt: Antifragile argues that some systems benefit from volatility, stress, disorder, and uncertainty. Taleb distinguishes fragile, robust, and antifragile systems and favors optionality over prediction.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Atomic Habits — Key Ideas (James Clear)
nodes.label: Atomic Habits — Key Ideas (James Clear)
nodes.excerpt: Atomic Habits presents habit change as identity-aligned behavior shaped by cues, cravings, responses, and rewards. Its practical power is focusing on environment design and small repeatable systems.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Clean Code — Key Ideas (Robert C. Martin)
nodes.label: Clean Code — Key Ideas (Robert C. Martin)
nodes.excerpt: Clean Code argues that code should be readable, intentional, small, well-named, tested, and maintainable. Some advice is debated, but the book's central concern is reducing comprehension cost.

nodes.label: Deep Work — Key Ideas (Newport)
nodes.excerpt: Deep Work argues that cognitively demanding, distraction-free concentration is increasingly valuable and increasingly rare. Newport emphasizes deliberate practice, attention protection, scheduling, and reducing shallow work.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Designing Data-Intensive Applications — Key Ideas (Kleppmann)
nodes.label: Designing Data-Intensive Applications — Key Ideas (Kleppmann)
nodes.excerpt: Designing Data-Intensive Applications explains the tradeoffs behind storage, replication, partitioning, transactions, consistency, batch processing, stream processing, and distributed systems. It is a systems-thinking book for modern data infrastructure.

nodes.label: Drive — Key Ideas (Daniel Pink)
nodes.excerpt: Drive argues that durable motivation depends less on external carrots and sticks than on autonomy, mastery, and purpose. The useful takeaway is to design work, habits, and learning environments so people feel ownership, see progress, and connect effort to something meaningful.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Gödel, Escher, Bach — Key Ideas (Hofstadter)
nodes.label: Gödel, Escher, Bach — Key Ideas (Hofstadter)
nodes.excerpt: Gödel, Escher, Bach explores minds, meaning, recursion, self-reference, formal systems, music, art, and consciousness. Its central fascination is how strange loops can produce meaning from symbol manipulation.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Human Compatible — Key Ideas (Stuart Russell)
nodes.label: Human Compatible — Key Ideas (Stuart Russell)
nodes.excerpt: Human Compatible argues that AI should be designed around uncertainty about human preferences rather than fixed objectives. Russell reframes alignment as building systems that defer, learn, and remain corrigible.

nodes.label: Life 3.0 — Key Ideas (Tegmark)
nodes.excerpt: Life 3.0 explores artificial intelligence, consciousness, cosmic futures, and choices about beneficial AI. Tegmark frames intelligence as the ability to accomplish goals and asks what future humans want.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Man's Search for Meaning — Key Ideas (Viktor Frankl)
nodes.label: Man's Search for Meaning — Key Ideas (Viktor Frankl)
nodes.excerpt: Man's Search for Meaning argues that humans can endure extreme suffering more effectively when life remains connected to meaning, responsibility, love, or a future task. Its enduring value is not optimism, but the distinction between unavoidable pain and the stance one chooses toward it.

nodes.label: Meditations — Key Ideas (Marcus Aurelius)
nodes.excerpt: Meditations is Marcus Aurelius's private Stoic practice journal about discipline, mortality, duty, perception, and control. Its value is as a repeated reminder to separate events from judgments and act according to character rather than mood.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Poor Charlie's Almanack — Key Ideas (Munger)
nodes.label: Poor Charlie's Almanack — Key Ideas (Munger)
nodes.excerpt: Poor Charlie's Almanack collects Charlie Munger's ideas on multidisciplinary thinking, incentives, inversion, mental models, rationality, investing, and judgment. Its core message is to build worldly wisdom across disciplines.

nodes.label: Sapiens — Key Ideas (Harari)
nodes.excerpt: Sapiens tells a broad, interpretive history of humanity through cognitive, agricultural, imperial, scientific, and capitalist revolutions. Its most memorable idea is that shared fictions let large groups cooperate.

nodes.label: Superintelligence — Key Ideas (Bostrom)
nodes.excerpt: Superintelligence explores the possibility that machine intelligence could exceed human intelligence and create severe control problems. Bostrom focuses on takeoff scenarios, instrumental goals, alignment, and governance.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Almanack of Naval Ravikant — Key Ideas
nodes.label: The Almanack of Naval Ravikant — Key Ideas
nodes.excerpt: The Almanack of Naval Ravikant collects Naval's ideas about wealth, judgment, leverage, specific knowledge, accountability, happiness, and freedom. Its recurring theme is building a life around compounding judgment and autonomy.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Black Swan — Key Ideas (Taleb)
nodes.label: The Black Swan — Key Ideas (Taleb)
nodes.excerpt: The Black Swan examines rare, high-impact, hard-to-predict events and the human tendency to explain them afterward. Taleb warns against overconfidence, naive models, and hidden tail risk.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Body Keeps the Score — Key Ideas (van der Kolk)
nodes.label: The Body Keeps the Score — Key Ideas (van der Kolk)
nodes.excerpt: The Body Keeps the Score argues that trauma affects the brain, body, memory, emotion, and relationships. It popularized body-aware trauma treatment, though specific interventions should be interpreted through current clinical evidence.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Hard Thing About Hard Things — Key Ideas (Horowitz)
nodes.label: The Hard Thing About Hard Things — Key Ideas (Horowitz)
nodes.excerpt: The Hard Thing About Hard Things is Ben Horowitz's practical account of leading companies through fear, layoffs, crisis, ambiguity, and imperfect decisions. It emphasizes that leadership is often about surviving non-ideal reality.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Innovator's Dilemma — Key Ideas (Christensen)
nodes.label: The Innovator's Dilemma — Key Ideas (Christensen)
nodes.excerpt: The Innovator's Dilemma explains how successful companies can fail by rationally serving current customers and profit models while disruptive entrants improve from overlooked markets. The threat is structural, not stupidity.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Power of Now — Key Ideas (Eckhart Tolle)
nodes.label: The Power of Now — Key Ideas (Eckhart Tolle)
nodes.excerpt: The Power of Now frames suffering as identification with compulsive thought, regret, anticipation, and ego narratives. Its practical value is the reminder to return attention to immediate experience when mental loops become louder than reality.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Pragmatic Programmer — Key Ideas (Hunt, Thomas)
nodes.label: The Pragmatic Programmer — Key Ideas (Hunt, Thomas)
nodes.excerpt: The Pragmatic Programmer is a practical software craftsmanship book about responsibility, feedback, automation, orthogonality, testing, debugging, and continuous learning. Its core message is to act like an owner of the work.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Selfish Gene — Key Ideas (Dawkins)
nodes.label: The Selfish Gene — Key Ideas (Dawkins)
nodes.excerpt: The Selfish Gene popularized a gene-centered view of evolution, explaining behavior through replication, selection, kinship, altruism, and memes. The title is metaphorical: genes are not conscious agents.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Staff Engineer's Path — Key Ideas (Reilly)
nodes.label: The Staff Engineer's Path — Key Ideas (Reilly)
nodes.excerpt: The Staff Engineer's Path explains senior individual-contributor leadership through scope, influence, strategy, execution, mentorship, and visibility. It helps engineers grow without assuming management is the only path.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/The Structure of Scientific Revolutions — Key Ideas (Kuhn)
nodes.label: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions — Key Ideas (Kuhn)
nodes.excerpt: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions argues that science often develops through paradigms, normal science, anomalies, crises, and revolutionary shifts. Kuhn changed how people discuss scientific progress.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Thinking Fast and Slow — Key Ideas (Kahneman)
nodes.label: Thinking Fast and Slow — Key Ideas (Kahneman)
nodes.excerpt: Thinking, Fast and Slow presents Daniel Kahneman's distinction between fast intuitive judgment and slower deliberate reasoning. Its enduring value is showing how human judgment can be useful, biased, confident, and wrong.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Thinking in Systems — Key Ideas (Meadows)
nodes.label: Thinking in Systems — Key Ideas (Meadows)
nodes.excerpt: Thinking in Systems teaches how to understand stocks, flows, feedback loops, delays, leverage points, and unintended consequences. It is a practical guide to seeing behavior as the product of system structure.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Why We Sleep — Key Ideas (Matthew Walker)
nodes.label: Why We Sleep — Key Ideas (Matthew Walker)
nodes.excerpt: Why We Sleep popularized the importance of sleep for memory, emotion, metabolism, immunity, performance, and health. Some specific claims have been debated, but the broad message that sleep is foundational remains useful.

nodes.id: Resources/Books & Ideas/Zero to One — Key Ideas (Thiel)
nodes.label: Zero to One — Key Ideas (Thiel)
nodes.excerpt: Zero to One argues that startups create the most value by building something new and defensible rather than competing in crowded markets. Its key lens is monopoly-like differentiation through unique insight.

nodes.label: Blue Ocean Strategy
nodes.excerpt: Blue Ocean Strategy argues that companies can escape direct competition by creating new demand and redefining value curves. The goal is not merely differentiation, but making old tradeoffs less relevant.

nodes.label: Brand Building
nodes.excerpt: Brand building creates memory, trust, differentiation, and preference over time. A strong brand reduces perceived risk, makes choices easier, and can compound distribution and pricing power.

nodes.label: Business Model Canvas
nodes.excerpt: The Business Model Canvas maps how a business creates, delivers, and captures value through customer segments, value propositions, channels, relationships, revenue, resources, activities, partners, and costs.

nodes.id: Resources/Business & Strategy/Competitive Moats (Network Effects, Switching Costs, Scale, IP)
nodes.label: Competitive Moats (Network Effects, Switching Costs, Scale, IP)
nodes.excerpt: Competitive moats are durable advantages that protect a company from competitors. Common moats include network effects, switching costs, scale economies, brand, data, regulation, distribution, and intellectual property.

nodes.label: First-Mover vs Fast-Follower
nodes.excerpt: First movers can gain learning, brand, data, and network advantages, but fast followers can avoid early mistakes and enter once demand is clearer. Timing advantage depends on moats, switching costs, capital, and execution.

nodes.label: Go-to-Market Strategy
nodes.excerpt: Go-to-market strategy defines how a company reaches, sells to, onboards, and retains a target customer segment. It connects positioning, channels, pricing, sales motion, lifecycle, and success metrics.

nodes.label: North Star Metric
nodes.excerpt: A North Star Metric is a single product or business metric intended to represent delivered customer value and long-term growth. It works only when paired with guardrails and input metrics.

nodes.id: Resources/Business & Strategy/Platform vs Pipeline Business Models
nodes.label: Platform vs Pipeline Business Models
nodes.excerpt: Pipeline businesses create value through a linear chain, while platform businesses facilitate interactions among external participants. Platforms often depend on network effects, governance, trust, and balancing multiple sides.

nodes.label: Porter's Five Forces
nodes.excerpt: Porter's Five Forces analyzes industry attractiveness through rivalry, buyer power, supplier power, threat of substitutes, and threat of new entrants. It explains profit pressure beyond a company's internal execution.

nodes.label: SWOT Analysis
nodes.excerpt: SWOT analysis organizes strategy around internal strengths and weaknesses plus external opportunities and threats. It is useful as a quick scan, but weak if it becomes a generic list without decisions.

nodes.id: Resources/Business & Strategy/Subscription Business Metrics (MRR, ARR, NRR, GRR)
nodes.label: Subscription Business Metrics (MRR, ARR, NRR, GRR)
nodes.excerpt: Subscription metrics track recurring revenue health through MRR, ARR, churn, retention, NRR, GRR, expansion, and cohort behavior. They reveal whether growth is durable or leaking.

nodes.label: The Innovator's Dilemma (Clayton Christensen)
nodes.excerpt: The Innovator's Dilemma explains how successful incumbents can miss disruptive innovations because they rationally serve their best customers and margins. Disruptors often begin in overlooked, low-end, or new-market segments.

nodes.label: The Strategy of Precommitment
nodes.excerpt: Precommitment is a strategy of binding future choices to make a current signal credible or prevent later temptation. It appears in negotiation, game theory, personal behavior, product strategy, and institutional design.

nodes.label: Vertical vs Horizontal Integration
nodes.excerpt: Vertical integration expands control across a supply chain, while horizontal integration expands across similar products, customers, or competitors. Each changes coordination, margin capture, flexibility, and market power.

nodes.id: Resources/Business & Strategy/Zero to One — Key Ideas (Peter Thiel)
nodes.label: Zero to One — Key Ideas (Peter Thiel)
nodes.excerpt: Zero to One argues that great startups create new value rather than merely competing in existing markets. Key ideas include monopoly profits, secrets, definite optimism, technology leverage, and starting with a small market.

nodes.label: CI CD
nodes.excerpt: CI/CD pipeline stages, GitHub Actions (OIDC, caching, matrix builds), deployment strategies (blue-green, canary, rolling, feature flags), Terraform, ArgoCD/GitOps.

nodes.label: Amortized Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation over a worst-case sequence, without relying on randomness. It explains why occasional expensive operations can still produce efficient long-run behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Arrays and Dynamic Arrays
nodes.label: Arrays and Dynamic Arrays
nodes.excerpt: Arrays store elements contiguously for fast index access, while dynamic arrays resize as they grow. They are simple, cache-friendly, and foundational for many data structures and algorithm patterns.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Automata Theory Basics (DFA, NFA)
nodes.label: Automata Theory Basics (DFA, NFA)
nodes.excerpt: Automata theory studies abstract machines that recognize patterns in strings. Deterministic and nondeterministic finite automata model regular languages and provide the theory behind many regex and parser ideas.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/B-Trees and B+ Trees
nodes.label: B-Trees and B+ Trees
nodes.excerpt: B-trees and B+ trees are balanced multiway search trees optimized for storage systems. They reduce disk and page reads by keeping many keys per node and shallow tree height.

nodes.label: Backtracking
nodes.excerpt: Backtracking explores possible choices depth-first and abandons branches that cannot lead to valid solutions. It is useful for permutations, combinations, constraint satisfaction, puzzles, and exhaustive search with pruning.

nodes.label: Bit Manipulation Tricks
nodes.excerpt: Bit manipulation uses binary operations to represent sets, flags, masks, parity, and low-level arithmetic efficiently. Common tricks rely on AND, OR, XOR, shifts, complements, and two's-complement behavior.

nodes.label: Bloom Filters
nodes.excerpt: Bloom filters are compact probabilistic data structures for membership checks. They can say an item is definitely not present or possibly present, trading false positives for memory efficiency.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Computational Complexity Classes (P, NP, NP-Hard)
nodes.label: Computational Complexity Classes (P, NP, NP-Hard)
nodes.excerpt: Computational complexity classes group problems by the resources needed to solve or verify them. P, NP, and NP-hard are central categories for reasoning about algorithmic tractability.

nodes.label: Divide and Conquer
nodes.excerpt: Divide and conquer solves problems by splitting them into smaller subproblems, solving those recursively, and combining the results. Merge sort, quicksort, binary search, and many tree algorithms use this pattern.

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming Patterns
nodes.excerpt: Dynamic programming solves problems with overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure by storing reusable results. Common patterns include one-dimensional DP, grid DP, knapsack, interval DP, tree DP, and state-machine DP.

nodes.label: Graph Traversal (BFS, DFS)
nodes.excerpt: Graph traversal systematically visits nodes and edges. Breadth-first search explores by distance layers, while depth-first search explores as far as possible before backtracking.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Graphs — Representations (Adjacency List vs Matrix)
nodes.label: Graphs — Representations (Adjacency List vs Matrix)
nodes.excerpt: Graph representation determines how efficiently algorithms inspect neighbors, edges, and connectivity. Adjacency lists are space-efficient for sparse graphs, while adjacency matrices make edge lookup simple but cost more memory.

nodes.label: Greedy Algorithms
nodes.excerpt: Greedy algorithms make the locally best choice at each step and hope it leads to a global optimum. They are fast and elegant when a proof shows the greedy choice is safe.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Hash Tables — Collision Resolution (Chaining, Open Addressing)
nodes.label: Hash Tables — Collision Resolution (Chaining, Open Addressing)
nodes.excerpt: Hash tables map keys to values using a hash function and an array of buckets. Collision resolution strategies such as chaining and open addressing determine how the table behaves when multiple keys land in the same place.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Heaps and Priority Queues
nodes.label: Heaps and Priority Queues
nodes.excerpt: Heaps are tree-shaped structures that efficiently expose the minimum or maximum element. Priority queues use heaps to process items by priority, supporting scheduling, shortest paths, streaming top-k, and event simulation.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular)
nodes.label: Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular)
nodes.excerpt: Linked lists store elements as nodes connected by pointers rather than contiguous memory. They make local insertion and deletion cheap when the node is known, but sacrifice cache locality and fast random access.

nodes.label: Memoization vs Tabulation
nodes.excerpt: Memoization and tabulation are two dynamic programming implementation styles. Memoization caches recursive calls top-down, while tabulation fills a table bottom-up in dependency order.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Trees (Kruskal, Prim)
nodes.label: Minimum Spanning Trees (Kruskal, Prim)
nodes.excerpt: Minimum spanning tree algorithms connect all vertices in a weighted undirected graph with minimum total edge cost and no cycles. Kruskal and Prim solve the same problem using different greedy strategies.

nodes.label: NP-Completeness Overview
nodes.excerpt: NP-completeness identifies problems that are both in NP and at least as hard as every problem in NP. It explains why many problems appear to resist efficient exact algorithms.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Recursion and Stack Frames
nodes.label: Recursion and Stack Frames
nodes.excerpt: Recursion solves problems by having a function call itself on smaller inputs, with stack frames tracking each active call. Understanding base cases, call stacks, and return flow prevents infinite recursion and stack overflow.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Regular Expressions — Theory and Practice
nodes.label: Regular Expressions — Theory and Practice
nodes.excerpt: Regular expressions describe string patterns, originally corresponding to regular languages. Practical regex engines add features and tradeoffs that make regex powerful, useful, and sometimes dangerous for performance and readability.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Searching Algorithms (Binary Search and Variants)
nodes.label: Searching Algorithms (Binary Search and Variants)
nodes.excerpt: Searching algorithms locate items or boundaries in a data space. Binary search and its variants exploit monotonic structure to cut the search space in half repeatedly.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Segment Trees and Fenwick Trees
nodes.label: Segment Trees and Fenwick Trees
nodes.excerpt: Segment trees and Fenwick trees answer range queries while supporting updates efficiently. They are specialized data structures for sums, minimums, maximums, frequencies, and prefix-based calculations.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A-star, Floyd-Warshall)
nodes.label: Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A-star, Floyd-Warshall)
nodes.excerpt: Shortest path algorithms find low-cost routes through graphs. Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A*, and Floyd-Warshall differ in edge-weight assumptions, negative-weight support, heuristics, and all-pairs versus single-source scope.

nodes.label: Skip Lists
nodes.excerpt: Skip lists maintain sorted order using layered linked lists with randomized promotion. They provide expected logarithmic search, insertion, and deletion while staying simpler than many balanced tree implementations.

nodes.label: Sliding Window Technique
nodes.excerpt: The sliding window technique maintains a contiguous range over an array or string while expanding and shrinking boundaries. It is useful for substring, subarray, frequency, and running-aggregate problems.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms — Comparison-Based (Merge, Quick, Heap, Tim)
nodes.label: Sorting Algorithms — Comparison-Based (Merge, Quick, Heap, Tim)
nodes.excerpt: Comparison-based sorting orders items using pairwise comparisons. Merge sort, quicksort, heap sort, and Timsort differ in stability, memory use, worst-case behavior, cache behavior, and real-world performance.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms — Non-Comparison (Radix, Counting, Bucket)
nodes.label: Sorting Algorithms — Non-Comparison (Radix, Counting, Bucket)
nodes.excerpt: Non-comparison sorting algorithms use assumptions about keys instead of only pairwise comparisons. Radix, counting, and bucket sort can beat comparison-sort bounds when value ranges, digit structure, or distribution assumptions are favorable.

nodes.label: Stacks and Queues
nodes.excerpt: Stacks and queues are linear data structures with constrained access patterns. Stacks use last-in-first-out behavior, while queues use first-in-first-out behavior, making them useful for parsing, traversal, scheduling, and buffering.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/String Matching Algorithms (KMP, Rabin-Karp)
nodes.label: String Matching Algorithms (KMP, Rabin-Karp)
nodes.excerpt: String matching algorithms find occurrences of a pattern inside text. KMP uses prefix structure to avoid backing up, while Rabin-Karp uses rolling hashes to compare candidate windows efficiently.

nodes.label: Topological Sort
nodes.excerpt: Topological sort orders nodes in a directed acyclic graph so every dependency comes before the thing that depends on it. It is useful for build systems, course schedules, task planning, and dependency resolution.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Trees — Binary, BST, AVL, Red-Black
nodes.label: Trees — Binary, BST, AVL, Red-Black
nodes.excerpt: Trees are hierarchical data structures made of nodes and parent-child relationships. Binary trees, search trees, AVL trees, and red-black trees trade structure and balancing rules for efficient lookup, insertion, deletion, and ordered traversal.

nodes.label: Tries (Prefix Trees)
nodes.excerpt: Tries store strings by sharing common prefixes across paths in a tree. They are useful for autocomplete, prefix queries, dictionaries, routing tables, and problems where prefix structure matters more than full-key hashing.

nodes.label: Two-Pointer Technique
nodes.excerpt: The two-pointer technique uses two indices or references that move through data in coordinated ways. It is common for sorted arrays, linked lists, pair searches, partitioning, and cycle detection.

nodes.label: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
nodes.excerpt: Union-Find, or Disjoint Set Union, tracks which elements belong to the same connected component. Path compression and union by rank make repeated union and find operations nearly constant time in practice.

nodes.label: Caching
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: cache layers, read/write strategies, eviction policies, stampede/penetration/avalanche problems, Redis internals.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Alcohol Metabolism (Ethanol to Acetaldehyde to Acetate)
nodes.label: Alcohol Metabolism (Ethanol to Acetaldehyde to Acetate)
nodes.excerpt: Alcohol metabolism primarily converts ethanol to acetaldehyde and then acetate, mostly in the liver. Acetaldehyde is toxic, and metabolism affects sleep, inflammation, liver stress, cancer risk, and hangover symptoms.

nodes.label: Biochemistry — Proteins and Enzymes
nodes.excerpt: Proteins are amino-acid polymers that fold into functional structures, and enzymes are proteins or RNA molecules that catalyze reactions. Protein shape, binding sites, and environmental conditions determine function.

nodes.label: Caffeine Mechanism (Adenosine Antagonism)
nodes.excerpt: Caffeine mainly works by blocking adenosine receptors, reducing the feeling of sleep pressure and increasing alertness. It does not erase sleep debt, and its half-life makes timing important.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Chemical Bonding (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen, Van der Waals)
nodes.label: Chemical Bonding (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen, Van der Waals)
nodes.excerpt: Chemical bonding explains how atoms and molecules stick together through electron sharing, electron transfer, polarity, and weak intermolecular forces. Ionic, covalent, hydrogen, and van der Waals interactions shape chemistry and biology.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/DNA and RNA — Structure and Function
nodes.label: DNA and RNA — Structure and Function
nodes.excerpt: DNA stores genetic information in a double-stranded nucleotide sequence, while RNA helps copy, regulate, and express that information. DNA, RNA, and proteins form the core information flow of biology.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/How Drugs Affect the Brain (Receptor Mechanisms)
nodes.label: How Drugs Affect the Brain (Receptor Mechanisms)
nodes.excerpt: Brain-active drugs change neural signaling by interacting with receptors, transporters, enzymes, and ion channels. Effects depend on target, dose, timing, brain region, tolerance, individual biology, and context.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Metabolic Pathways (Glycolysis, Krebs Cycle, Electron Transport Chain)
nodes.label: Metabolic Pathways (Glycolysis, Krebs Cycle, Electron Transport Chain)
nodes.excerpt: Metabolic pathways transform nutrients into usable energy and cellular building blocks. Glycolysis, the Krebs cycle, and the electron transport chain are central to ATP production.

nodes.label: Nootropics — Evidence-Based Overview
nodes.excerpt: Nootropics are substances marketed or used to improve cognition, but evidence, safety, regulation, and effect size vary widely. Sleep, exercise, treatment of real disorders, caffeine, and creatine have stronger grounding than most supplement stacks.

nodes.label: Organic Chemistry Basics
nodes.excerpt: Organic chemistry studies carbon-containing molecules, functional groups, structure, reactivity, and mechanisms. It matters because proteins, fats, carbohydrates, DNA, drugs, and many materials are organic chemistry in action.

nodes.label: Periodic Table Essentials
nodes.excerpt: The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number, electron structure, and recurring chemical properties. Its rows and columns reveal patterns in reactivity, bonding, size, electronegativity, and metallic character.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Pharmacology Basics — How Drugs Work
nodes.label: Pharmacology Basics — How Drugs Work
nodes.excerpt: Pharmacology studies how drugs interact with the body through absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, receptors, dose-response, efficacy, potency, and side effects. Effects depend on both molecule and context.

nodes.label: Protein Synthesis (Transcription and Translation)
nodes.excerpt: Protein synthesis turns genetic information into proteins through transcription and translation. DNA is transcribed into RNA, then ribosomes translate mRNA codons into amino-acid chains.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/The Chemistry of Sleep (Melatonin, Adenosine, GABA)
nodes.label: The Chemistry of Sleep (Melatonin, Adenosine, GABA)
nodes.excerpt: Sleep chemistry involves timing signals like melatonin, sleep-pressure signals like adenosine, inhibitory signaling through GABA, and many interacting systems. No single chemical explains sleep by itself.

nodes.label: Claude Code Skills
nodes.excerpt: how Claude Code skills work: markdown files injected into context, gstack architecture (templates + Bun build), gbrain per-project learnings, how to write your own skill.

nodes.label: Cloud Cost Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, Spot, right-sizing, Graviton, auto scaling patterns, S3 lifecycle, NAT Gateway mitigation, data transfer costs, tagging, Cost Explorer.

nodes.label: Cloud Networking
nodes.excerpt: VPC, subnets, IGW, NAT Gateway, security groups, VPC peering (non-transitive), Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke), VPN vs Direct Connect, PrivateLink, data transfer pricing.

nodes.label: Cloud Services
nodes.excerpt: AWS and GCP services: EC2/GCE, Lambda/Cloud Functions, S3/GCS, EBS, RDS/Aurora, DynamoDB/Firestore, ElastiCache, SQS/SNS/Pub-Sub, ECS/EKS/GKE/Cloud Run, CloudFront, ALB/NLB, IAM, CloudWatch.

nodes.label: Anchoring Bias
nodes.excerpt: Anchoring bias occurs when an initial number, frame, or example pulls later judgments toward it. It affects negotiation, estimation, prioritization, design discussions, and planning.

nodes.label: Availability Heuristic
nodes.excerpt: The availability heuristic estimates likelihood based on how easily examples come to mind. Vivid, recent, emotional, or repeated examples can make rare events feel common or common events feel invisible.

nodes.label: Baader-Meinhof Phenomenon (Frequency Illusion)
nodes.excerpt: The Baader-Meinhof phenomenon, or frequency illusion, happens when something newly noticed suddenly seems everywhere. Attention and memory make the pattern more visible, not necessarily more frequent.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Cognitive Biases — Complete Reference
nodes.label: Cognitive Biases — Complete Reference
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive biases are systematic tendencies in perception, memory, attention, and judgment that can distort reasoning. They are not personal defects; they are predictable shortcuts that need awareness, feedback, and better decision processes.

nodes.label: Cognitive Load Theory
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive load theory explains learning in terms of limited working memory and schema formation. Good instruction reduces unnecessary load, manages complexity, and helps learners build durable mental structures.

nodes.label: Confirmation Bias
nodes.excerpt: Confirmation bias is the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember evidence that supports existing beliefs. It is especially dangerous when identity, ego, or sunk cost is attached to being right.

nodes.label: Decision Fatigue
nodes.excerpt: Decision fatigue is the decline in decision quality after repeated cognitive effort, uncertainty, or self-control demands. Reducing low-value choices preserves attention for decisions that matter.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Dual Process Theory (System 1 vs System 2)
nodes.label: Dual Process Theory (System 1 vs System 2)
nodes.excerpt: Dual process theory distinguishes fast, automatic, intuitive processing from slower, effortful, analytical processing. The System 1/System 2 framing is useful, but simplified and should not be treated as literal separate brains.

nodes.label: Dunning-Kruger Effect
