nodes.excerpt: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how low skill can impair the ability to accurately judge skill, leading to overconfidence. The broader lesson is that calibration depends on feedback and domain knowledge.

nodes.label: Fundamental Attribution Error
nodes.excerpt: Fundamental attribution error is overemphasizing personality and underemphasizing situation when explaining other people's behavior. It can make mistakes look like character flaws instead of context, incentives, or constraints.

nodes.label: Heuristics in Decision-Making
nodes.excerpt: Heuristics are mental shortcuts that make decisions faster under uncertainty. They are useful when matched to the environment and dangerous when mistaken for complete analysis.

nodes.label: In-Group - Out-Group Bias
nodes.excerpt: In-group and out-group bias makes people favor their own group and interpret outsiders more negatively. It shapes trust, conflict, hiring, communities, politics, and team dynamics.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Inductive vs Deductive vs Abductive Reasoning
nodes.label: Inductive vs Deductive vs Abductive Reasoning
nodes.excerpt: Deductive reasoning derives necessary conclusions from premises, inductive reasoning generalizes from observations, and abductive reasoning infers the best explanation. Good problem-solving often uses all three.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Logical Fallacies — Full List
nodes.label: Logical Fallacies — Full List
nodes.excerpt: Logical fallacies are flawed argument patterns that weaken reasoning, such as straw man, ad hominem, false dilemma, circular reasoning, slippery slope, and appeal to authority. They are useful for analysis, but can be misused as debate weapons.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Loss Aversion (Prospect Theory — Kahneman and Tversky)
nodes.label: Loss Aversion (Prospect Theory — Kahneman and Tversky)
nodes.excerpt: Loss aversion is the tendency for losses to feel more psychologically powerful than equivalent gains. In prospect theory, people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point rather than pure final wealth.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Mental Models — Full Reference
nodes.label: Mental Models — Full Reference
nodes.excerpt: Mental models are reusable thinking tools for explaining systems, predicting outcomes, and making decisions. A strong model library includes technical, psychological, economic, probabilistic, and systems models.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Nudge Theory (Thaler and Sunstein)
nodes.label: Nudge Theory (Thaler and Sunstein)
nodes.excerpt: Nudge theory studies how choice architecture changes behavior without forbidding options. Defaults, friction, ordering, reminders, and framing can guide choices, for good or manipulative ends.

nodes.label: Optimism Bias
nodes.excerpt: Optimism bias is the tendency to overestimate positive outcomes and underestimate negative ones for oneself or one's plan. It can support motivation, but needs risk checks when decisions are expensive.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Overfitting in Human Reasoning
nodes.label: Overfitting in Human Reasoning
nodes.excerpt: Overfitting in human reasoning is building an explanation too tightly around limited examples, noise, or recent experience. The result feels insightful but fails to generalize.

nodes.label: Planning Fallacy
nodes.excerpt: The planning fallacy is underestimating how long tasks will take despite knowing similar tasks have taken longer before. The outside view, buffers, and historical data are the main antidotes.

nodes.label: Recency Bias
nodes.excerpt: Recency bias overweights recent information relative to older but still relevant evidence. It affects hiring, investing, debugging, sports judgment, product decisions, and self-evaluation.

nodes.label: Status Quo Bias
nodes.excerpt: Status quo bias is the preference for existing conditions simply because they are familiar or default. It can protect stability, but also preserves outdated systems, habits, and assumptions.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Streetlight Effect (Looking Where It Is Easy)
nodes.label: Streetlight Effect (Looking Where It Is Easy)
nodes.excerpt: The streetlight effect is looking for answers where measurement is easiest rather than where the answer is most likely. It shows up in analytics, debugging, research, and productivity metrics.

nodes.label: Sunk Cost Fallacy
nodes.excerpt: The sunk cost fallacy is continuing a choice because of past investment rather than future value. Money, time, effort, identity, and public commitment can all make quitting feel irrationally hard.

nodes.label: Survivorship Bias
nodes.excerpt: Survivorship bias happens when analysis focuses only on examples that made it through a selection process. It distorts lessons from startups, careers, investing, fitness, history, and engineering incidents.

nodes.label: Active Listening
nodes.excerpt: Active listening is the practice of giving full attention, reflecting meaning, asking clarifying questions, and checking understanding. It improves trust, conflict resolution, coaching, research, and collaboration.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Async vs Sync Communication — When to Use Each
nodes.label: Async vs Sync Communication — When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: Async communication is best for durable context, updates, decisions, and focused work, while synchronous communication is best for ambiguity, conflict, brainstorming, urgency, and relationship repair. The right mode depends on bandwidth and stakes.

nodes.label: Conflict Resolution Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Conflict resolution turns disagreement into clearer interests, options, boundaries, and decisions. Useful strategies include de-escalation, active listening, reframing, separating people from problems, and choosing the right forum.

nodes.label: Email Etiquette and Efficiency
nodes.excerpt: Email etiquette and efficiency depend on clear subjects, explicit asks, concise context, appropriate recipients, and respectful response expectations. Good email reduces coordination cost.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Explaining Complex Ideas Simply (The Curse of Knowledge)
nodes.label: Explaining Complex Ideas Simply (The Curse of Knowledge)
nodes.excerpt: Explaining complex ideas simply requires noticing the curse of knowledge: experts forget what beginners do not know. Good explanation uses audience models, concrete examples, progressive disclosure, and checked understanding.

nodes.label: Negotiation Fundamentals (BATNA, ZOPA)
nodes.excerpt: Negotiation fundamentals include interests, alternatives, BATNA, reservation price, ZOPA, anchors, concessions, and relationship dynamics. Strong negotiators improve their alternatives and understand the other side's constraints.

nodes.label: Non-Violent Communication (NVC — Marshall Rosenberg)
nodes.excerpt: Non-Violent Communication frames difficult conversations through observations, feelings, needs, and requests. It tries to reduce blame and increase mutual understanding without avoiding boundaries.

nodes.label: Persuasion — Cialdini's Six Principles
nodes.excerpt: Cialdini's persuasion principles describe common influence patterns: reciprocity, commitment and consistency, social proof, authority, liking, and scarcity. They are useful for understanding influence and for noticing manipulation.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Presentation Design (Assertion-Evidence vs Bullet Slides)
nodes.label: Presentation Design (Assertion-Evidence vs Bullet Slides)
nodes.excerpt: Presentation design improves audience understanding by replacing vague topic slides with clear assertions supported by visual evidence. Assertion-evidence slides make the message explicit and reduce cognitive burden.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Public Speaking — Fear and Techniques
nodes.label: Public Speaking — Fear and Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Public speaking improves through preparation, audience focus, structure, rehearsal, breathing, exposure, and recovery from small mistakes. Fear is normal; skill comes from reducing uncertainty and increasing reps.

nodes.label: Rhetoric — Logos, Ethos, Pathos
nodes.excerpt: Rhetoric studies persuasive communication through logic, credibility, emotion, audience, structure, and style. Logos, ethos, and pathos are classic lenses for how arguments persuade.

nodes.label: Storytelling with Data
nodes.excerpt: Storytelling with data turns analysis into a clear argument through audience focus, chart choice, annotation, context, sequencing, and visual emphasis. The goal is insight and decision support, not decorative charts.

nodes.label: Technical Writing Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Technical writing explains systems, procedures, decisions, and concepts so readers can understand and act. Good technical writing is accurate, structured, audience-aware, searchable, and maintained.

nodes.label: The Pyramid Principle (Barbara Minto)
nodes.excerpt: The Pyramid Principle structures communication by leading with the answer, then supporting it with grouped, logically ordered arguments. It is useful for executive summaries, strategy memos, and decision communication.

nodes.label: Writing Clearly — Principles (Williams, Pinker)
nodes.excerpt: Clear writing makes ideas easier to understand by reducing ambiguity, needless abstraction, weak structure, and reader effort. Principles from Joseph Williams and Steven Pinker emphasize concrete subjects, strong verbs, coherence, and reader empathy.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Writing for Developers — Docs, READMEs, Commit Messages
nodes.label: Writing for Developers — Docs, READMEs, Commit Messages
nodes.excerpt: Writing for developers includes docs, READMEs, commit messages, pull request descriptions, comments, and decision records. Good developer writing preserves intent, reduces onboarding cost, and improves collaboration.

nodes.label: DSA Practice
nodes.excerpt: DSA patterns by category (arrays, trees, graphs, DP, backtracking, binary search) with spaced repetition notes.

nodes.label: Data Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ELT vs ETL, data lake (Parquet, Iceberg, Medallion architecture), dbt, Airflow/Dagster, Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift, Spark, Flink, Kinesis, Glue, CDC/Debezium, reverse ETL.

nodes.label: A-B Testing Design
nodes.excerpt: A/B testing compares product or system variants using randomized assignment and statistical analysis. Good test design defines the hypothesis, unit of randomization, metrics, sample size, guardrails, and stopping rules before launch.

nodes.label: Anomaly Detection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Anomaly detection finds observations or patterns that differ meaningfully from expected behavior. Useful methods range from simple thresholds to statistical baselines, clustering, isolation forests, and time-series models.

nodes.label: Bayesian Statistics
nodes.excerpt: Bayesian statistics treats probability as a degree of belief updated by evidence. It combines prior beliefs with observed data to produce posterior beliefs.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.label: Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.excerpt: Causal inference asks whether an action or exposure caused an outcome, not merely whether two variables moved together. It uses experiments, assumptions, controls, and quasi-experimental methods to reason about cause and effect.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.label: Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.excerpt: Clustering groups unlabeled data points by similarity. K-means, DBSCAN, and hierarchical clustering each make different assumptions about cluster shape, density, and how many groups exist.

nodes.label: Cohort Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Cohort analysis groups users or entities by a shared start condition and tracks behavior over time. It is especially useful for retention, activation, lifecycle, and product-quality questions.

nodes.label: Confidence Intervals
nodes.excerpt: Confidence intervals estimate a plausible range for an unknown population value from sample data. They communicate uncertainty better than a single point estimate, but they are often misread as probabilities about one fixed interval.

nodes.label: Dashboard Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Dashboard design organizes metrics so users can monitor status, diagnose changes, and act. Effective dashboards prioritize audience, metric definitions, hierarchy, freshness, comparisons, and drill-down paths.

nodes.label: Data Cleaning Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Data cleaning turns messy, inconsistent, duplicated, or invalid data into a reliable analysis dataset. It is less glamorous than modeling but often determines whether conclusions are trustworthy.

nodes.label: Data Lake Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Data lake architecture stores raw and processed data in scalable object storage for analytics, ML, and archival use. Its value depends on metadata, governance, file formats, partitioning, and transformation discipline.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.label: Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.excerpt: Data normalization and scaling transform numeric features into comparable ranges or distributions. They are essential for distance-based models, gradient-based optimization, and stable feature interpretation.

nodes.label: Data Warehousing Concepts
nodes.excerpt: Data warehousing organizes cleaned, queryable data for analytics and reporting. Core concepts include facts, dimensions, star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, marts, lineage, and governance.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.label: Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.excerpt: Dimensionality reduction compresses high-dimensional data into fewer dimensions for visualization, denoising, or modeling. PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP differ in whether they preserve global variance, local neighborhoods, or manifold structure.

nodes.label: ETL vs ELT
nodes.excerpt: ETL transforms data before loading it into a target store, while ELT loads raw data first and transforms it inside the warehouse or lakehouse. The choice affects governance, cost, flexibility, and pipeline ownership.

nodes.label: Feature Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Feature engineering turns raw data into model inputs that expose useful signal. It often matters more than model choice because features encode domain knowledge, time context, aggregations, and constraints.

nodes.label: Feature Selection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Feature selection chooses which variables to keep for modeling. It improves interpretability, reduces noise, lowers cost, and can prevent overfitting when done with proper validation.

nodes.label: Funnel Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Funnel analysis measures how users move through a sequence of steps toward a goal. It identifies drop-off points, conversion rates, and where product or process changes may matter most.

nodes.label: Handling Missing Data
nodes.excerpt: Handling missing data means understanding why values are absent and choosing a treatment that preserves valid inference. Deletion, imputation, missingness indicators, and model-aware methods each carry assumptions.

nodes.label: Hypothesis Testing
nodes.excerpt: Hypothesis testing evaluates whether observed data is surprising under a null hypothesis. It is useful for experiments, but it depends heavily on assumptions, sample size, and careful interpretation.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.label: Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.excerpt: Choosing metrics and KPIs means deciding what behavior the team should optimize and monitor. Strong metrics are aligned with goals, hard to game, clearly defined, and paired with guardrails.

nodes.label: OLAP vs OLTP
nodes.excerpt: OLTP systems handle live transactions, while OLAP systems support analytical queries over large historical datasets. Confusing the two leads to slow apps, expensive queries, and unreliable reporting.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.label: P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.excerpt: P-values measure how surprising observed data would be under a null hypothesis. Statistical significance is a thresholding convention, not proof that an effect is real, large, or important.

nodes.label: Probability Theory Basics
nodes.excerpt: Probability theory models uncertainty using events, random variables, distributions, expectation, and conditional probability. It underpins statistics, machine learning, experimentation, and risk reasoning.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.label: Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.excerpt: Regression analysis models relationships between inputs and an outcome. Linear, logistic, and polynomial regression are foundational tools for prediction, explanation, and estimating effects with assumptions.

nodes.label: SQL Window Functions
nodes.excerpt: SQL window functions compute values across related rows without collapsing the result set. They are essential for rankings, running totals, moving averages, retention analysis, and event-sequence queries.

nodes.label: SQL for Analytics
nodes.excerpt: SQL for analytics uses relational queries to answer business, product, and operational questions. The practical skill is joining, filtering, grouping, aggregating, and validating data without losing the meaning of the metric.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.label: Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.excerpt: Statistics fundamentals summarize data with measures of center and spread. Mean, median, mode, variance, and standard deviation are the starting vocabulary for reasoning about datasets.

nodes.label: Survival Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Survival analysis models time until an event occurs, such as churn, failure, conversion, or relapse. It handles censored data where the event has not happened yet during the observation window.

nodes.label: Time Series Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Time series analysis studies data ordered over time, such as metrics, demand, latency, or prices. It focuses on trend, seasonality, autocorrelation, forecasting, and anomaly detection.

nodes.label: Visualization Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Visualization best practices help charts communicate patterns without distorting the data. Good visualizations choose the right chart type, scale, labeling, ordering, color, and context for the question.

nodes.label: dbt (Data Build Tool)
nodes.excerpt: dbt is a transformation tool that lets analytics teams build versioned, tested SQL models in the warehouse. It brings software engineering practices such as modularity, lineage, documentation, and CI to analytics work.

nodes.label: Data Structures and Algorithms Patterns
nodes.excerpt: LeetCode/NeetCode pattern guide covering arrays, hash maps, two pointers, sliding windows, heaps, graphs, union find, backtracking, DP, and tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Database Internals Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: deeper database internals: pages, WAL, MVCC, vacuum/bloat, indexes, query planner, isolation anomalies, locks, replication, backups, partitioning, distributed SQL.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Database Backups and Point-in-Time Recovery
nodes.label: Database Backups and Point-in-Time Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Database backups are only useful if they can be restored, and point-in-time recovery uses backups plus logs to recover to a specific moment. Recovery planning should be tested, not assumed.

nodes.label: Database Change Data Capture (CDC)
nodes.excerpt: Change Data Capture streams database changes into downstream systems without requiring every application path to publish events manually. CDC is useful for analytics, search indexing, cache updates, replication, and event-driven integration.

nodes.label: Database Connection Pooling (pgBouncer, SQLAlchemy)
nodes.excerpt: Connection pooling reuses database connections so applications do not open expensive new sessions for every request. Tools like pgBouncer and SQLAlchemy pools protect the database from connection storms and improve latency.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Database Indexing Strategies (B-Tree, Hash, GIN, GiST)
nodes.label: Database Indexing Strategies (B-Tree, Hash, GIN, GiST)
nodes.excerpt: Database indexes are access structures tuned for different query shapes. B-tree indexes cover common equality and range queries, while hash, GIN, and GiST indexes support more specialized lookup patterns.

nodes.label: Database Normalization (1NF through BCNF)
nodes.excerpt: Database normalization reduces redundancy and update anomalies by decomposing tables according to dependency rules. The practical point is preserving facts once, in the right place, unless performance demands a documented exception.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Full-Text Search (PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, Typesense)
nodes.label: Full-Text Search (PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, Typesense)
nodes.excerpt: Full-text search indexes terms, rankings, and language-aware matching so users can find documents by meaning-adjacent text instead of exact values. PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, and Typesense cover different complexity levels.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Document Stores (MongoDB)
nodes.excerpt: Document stores organize data as flexible JSON-like documents, often optimizing for aggregate-oriented reads and schema evolution. MongoDB is the common reference point, but document databases still require intentional modeling.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Graph Databases (Neo4j)
nodes.excerpt: Graph databases model data as nodes, edges, and properties, making relationship traversal a first-class operation. They are strongest when the relationships are the product, not just supporting metadata.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Key-Value Stores (Redis)
nodes.excerpt: Key-value stores map keys directly to values and optimize for simple, fast lookups. Redis is the common example, often used for caching, counters, locks, queues, pub/sub, and rate limiting.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Wide-Column Stores (Cassandra)
nodes.excerpt: Wide-column stores like Cassandra organize data by partition keys and clustering columns for massive distributed writes and reads at known access patterns. They reward query-first modeling and punish ad hoc querying.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/ORM Patterns and Pitfalls (N+1 Problem, Lazy Loading)
nodes.label: ORM Patterns and Pitfalls (N+1 Problem, Lazy Loading)
nodes.excerpt: ORMs map database tables to application objects, improving ergonomics but sometimes hiding query cost. The N+1 problem, lazy loading surprises, and leaky abstractions are the classic failure modes.

nodes.label: Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking
nodes.excerpt: Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare and checks before commit, while pessimistic locking blocks concurrent writers earlier. The right choice depends on contention, user experience, and correctness requirements.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/PostgreSQL — Advanced Features (JSONB, Arrays, Extensions)
nodes.label: PostgreSQL — Advanced Features (JSONB, Arrays, Extensions)
nodes.excerpt: PostgreSQL goes beyond classic relational storage with JSONB, arrays, full-text search, extensions, custom types, and strong indexing. Its strength is being a reliable relational core that can absorb many adjacent workloads.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Redis Patterns (Caching, Pub Sub, Job Queues, Rate Limiting)
nodes.label: Redis Patterns (Caching, Pub Sub, Job Queues, Rate Limiting)
nodes.excerpt: Redis is commonly used as a cache, pub/sub broker, lightweight queue, distributed coordination tool, counter store, and rate limiter. The main engineering risk is treating every Redis pattern as equally durable or reliable.

nodes.label: Relational Database Design
nodes.excerpt: Relational database design models data as tables, rows, relationships, keys, and constraints. Good schema design makes invariants explicit so application code is not the only thing protecting correctness.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Indexes and Query Plans (EXPLAIN)
nodes.label: SQL — Indexes and Query Plans (EXPLAIN)
nodes.excerpt: Indexes give databases faster access paths, while query plans show how the optimizer intends to execute SQL. EXPLAIN turns performance work from guessing into inspection.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Joins (Inner, Left, Right, Full, Cross)
nodes.label: SQL — Joins (Inner, Left, Right, Full, Cross)
nodes.excerpt: SQL joins combine rows across tables using relationship predicates. Understanding inner, outer, and cross joins is essential for reading query intent and avoiding accidental row multiplication.

nodes.label: SQL — Subqueries and CTEs
nodes.excerpt: Subqueries and common table expressions break SQL logic into nested or named steps. They improve readability when used well, but can also hide expensive intermediate work.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Transactions and ACID Properties
nodes.label: SQL — Transactions and ACID Properties
nodes.excerpt: SQL transactions group work so it commits or rolls back as one unit. ACID captures atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability: the core guarantees behind reliable relational state changes.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQLite — Use Cases and Limits
nodes.label: SQLite — Use Cases and Limits
nodes.excerpt: SQLite is an embedded relational database stored in a local file. It is excellent for local apps, prototypes, edge deployments, tests, and small services, but it is not a drop-in replacement for client-server databases under high write concurrency.

nodes.label: Time Series Databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB)
nodes.excerpt: Time series databases optimize for timestamped measurements, retention policies, downsampling, and range queries over time. They fit metrics, IoT readings, financial ticks, logs, and operational telemetry.

nodes.label: Databases
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: ACID, MVCC, indexes (B-tree/hash/composite/covering), query planning, N+1, connection pooling, SQL vs all 4 NoSQL types, PostgreSQL specifics.

nodes.label: Accessibility in Design (WCAG 2.1, 2.2)
nodes.excerpt: Accessibility in design ensures products can be used by people with varied vision, hearing, motor, cognitive, and situational needs. WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 organize guidance around perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust experiences.

nodes.label: Color Theory Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Color theory covers hue, saturation, value, contrast, harmony, semantics, accessibility, and emotional association. In product design, color should support hierarchy, meaning, state, and legibility.

nodes.id: Resources/Design & UX/Dark Patterns — A Catalog of What to Avoid
nodes.label: Dark Patterns — A Catalog of What to Avoid
nodes.excerpt: Dark patterns are deceptive or manipulative interface designs that push users into choices they might not otherwise make. They can increase short-term metrics while damaging trust, autonomy, and sometimes legal compliance.

nodes.label: Design Handoff to Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Design handoff to engineering translates product intent, interaction states, edge cases, assets, accessibility requirements, and design tokens into buildable guidance. Good handoff is collaboration, not a file drop.

nodes.label: Design Systems Overview
nodes.excerpt: Design systems are shared foundations for product design and engineering, including components, tokens, patterns, guidelines, and governance. They improve consistency and speed when treated as living infrastructure.

nodes.label: Designing for Emotion
nodes.excerpt: Designing for emotion considers how products make people feel through clarity, control, trust, delight, safety, momentum, and identity. Emotional design should support user goals rather than manipulate them.

nodes.label: Figma Workflow and Components
nodes.excerpt: Figma workflows use files, pages, frames, components, variants, auto layout, styles, variables, libraries, and comments to support collaborative design. Good structure makes design easier to maintain and hand off.

nodes.label: Gestalt Principles in Design
nodes.excerpt: Gestalt principles describe how people perceive visual patterns as organized wholes. Proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, common region, and figure-ground help designers create clear visual grouping.

nodes.label: Grid Systems in Design
nodes.excerpt: Grid systems create layout structure through columns, rows, margins, gutters, spacing, and alignment. They help interfaces feel organized, scalable, and easier to scan.

nodes.label: Human-Centered Design Process
nodes.excerpt: Human-centered design starts with people's needs, contexts, constraints, and behaviors, then iterates through research, synthesis, ideation, prototyping, testing, and implementation. It treats design as learning, not decoration.

nodes.label: Information Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Information architecture organizes content, navigation, labels, and relationships so people can find and understand what they need. It is the structure beneath usable digital experiences.

nodes.id: Resources/Design & UX/Interaction Design Principles (Fitts's Law, Hick's Law, Jakob's Law)
nodes.label: Interaction Design Principles (Fitts's Law, Hick's Law, Jakob's Law)
nodes.excerpt: Interaction design principles explain how people perceive, choose, and act in interfaces. Fitts's Law, Hick's Law, and Jakob's Law are useful heuristics for targets, choices, and familiar patterns.

nodes.label: Micro-Interactions Design
nodes.excerpt: Micro-interactions are small product moments that handle feedback, status, affordance, confirmation, error prevention, and delight. They make interfaces feel responsive and understandable.

nodes.label: Mobile-First Design
nodes.excerpt: Mobile-first design starts with constrained screen space, touch interaction, performance, and core tasks before expanding to larger screens. It forces prioritization and helps create responsive experiences.

nodes.label: Motion Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Motion design uses animation and transition to clarify state changes, guide attention, express continuity, and provide feedback. Good motion supports understanding; excessive motion becomes noise or an accessibility problem.

nodes.label: The Aesthetic-Usability Effect
nodes.excerpt: The aesthetic-usability effect is the tendency for users to perceive attractive designs as easier to use. Visual polish can increase tolerance and trust, but it cannot fully compensate for broken usability.

nodes.label: Typography Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Typography shapes readability, hierarchy, tone, and information density through typeface choice, size, weight, line length, spacing, alignment, and contrast. Good typography makes content easier to use.

nodes.label: UX Research Methods Overview
nodes.excerpt: UX research methods help teams understand user needs, behavior, context, usability, and attitudes. Methods include interviews, surveys, usability tests, field studies, diary studies, analytics, and concept testing.

nodes.label: User Personas
nodes.excerpt: User personas are synthesized representations of target users, including goals, contexts, behaviors, needs, and constraints. They are useful when evidence-based and dangerous when invented as demographic stereotypes.

nodes.label: Wireframing vs Prototyping
nodes.excerpt: Wireframes sketch structure and information layout, while prototypes simulate interaction, flow, and behavior. Both reduce uncertainty, but they answer different design questions.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/AWS Core Services (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, VPC)
nodes.label: AWS Core Services (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, VPC)
nodes.excerpt: AWS core services map to common infrastructure primitives: EC2 for virtual machines, S3 for object storage, Lambda for functions, RDS for managed relational databases, and VPC for private networking. These services form the base vocabulary for many AWS architectures.

nodes.label: Ansible Basics
nodes.excerpt: Ansible automates configuration management, provisioning, and operational tasks over SSH or connection plugins. It is useful for making server state repeatable without requiring an agent on every machine.

nodes.label: Bash Scripting
nodes.excerpt: Bash scripting automates command-line workflows by composing programs, variables, conditionals, loops, functions, and exit codes. Good scripts are boring, explicit, quoted carefully, and written with failure behavior in mind.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Blue-Green vs Canary vs Rolling Deployments
nodes.label: Blue-Green vs Canary vs Rolling Deployments
nodes.excerpt: Blue-green, canary, and rolling deployments are rollout strategies for reducing release risk. They differ in infrastructure cost, speed, rollback behavior, traffic control, and how quickly user impact is detected.

nodes.label: CI-CD Pipeline Design
nodes.excerpt: CI/CD pipeline design automates building, testing, packaging, securing, and deploying software. A good pipeline is fast enough for developer feedback, strict enough to protect production, and observable enough to debug failures.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Cloud Providers Overview (AWS, GCP, Azure)
nodes.label: Cloud Providers Overview (AWS, GCP, Azure)
nodes.excerpt: AWS, GCP, and Azure provide managed compute, storage, networking, database, security, and analytics services. Choosing among them is usually about ecosystem fit, team familiarity, enterprise constraints, pricing shape, and managed-service maturity.

nodes.label: Cloudflare Services Overview
nodes.excerpt: Cloudflare provides edge services for DNS, CDN caching, DDoS mitigation, TLS, web application security, access control, workers, tunnels, and traffic routing. It is often used as the public edge in front of applications.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Cron Jobs and Task Scheduling
nodes.label: Cron Jobs and Task Scheduling
nodes.excerpt: Cron jobs and task schedulers run recurring work such as reports, cleanup, backups, imports, and maintenance. Reliable scheduling requires idempotency, locking, observability, retries, time-zone care, and clear ownership.

nodes.label: Database Migrations Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Database migration best practices let teams change schemas and data safely while applications keep running. The safest migrations are small, backward-compatible, observable, reversible where possible, and sequenced with application deployments.

nodes.label: Disk I-O Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Disk I/O optimization reduces latency and throughput bottlenecks in reads, writes, fsyncs, logs, databases, and storage systems. It requires understanding access patterns, caching, batching, indexes, queues, and storage-device limits.

nodes.label: Error Budgets
nodes.excerpt: An error budget is the allowed unreliability derived from an SLO: budget = (1 - SLO) × window. A 99.9% SLO allows 43.8 minutes of downtime per month. Burn rate measures how fast the budget is consumed; multi-window alerts (1h fast burn + 6h slow burn) catch both sudden outages and slow degradations. When budget is exhausted, reliability work takes priority over features. The mechanism that makes reliability a product decision, not an engineering complaint.

nodes.label: GitHub Actions Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: GitHub Actions is a workflow automation platform built around events, jobs, steps, runners, actions, secrets, and permissions. Using it well means controlling triggers, caches, credentials, artifacts, and least-privilege access.

nodes.label: GitOps Principles
nodes.excerpt: GitOps manages infrastructure and application delivery by storing desired state in Git and reconciling runtime systems to match it. It improves auditability, review, rollback, and drift detection when repository boundaries and permissions are designed well.

nodes.label: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
nodes.excerpt: Infrastructure as Code manages cloud and platform resources through versioned declarative or scripted definitions. It makes infrastructure reviewable, repeatable, auditable, and safer to change.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Kubernetes YAML — Deployment, Service, Ingress
nodes.label: Kubernetes YAML — Deployment, Service, Ingress
nodes.excerpt: Kubernetes YAML defines desired state for workloads, networking, and routing through objects such as Deployments, Services, and Ingresses. Reading these manifests means understanding selectors, pods, replicas, ports, health checks, and controller behavior.

nodes.label: Lambda Functions and FaaS
nodes.excerpt: Lambda functions and Function-as-a-Service platforms run short-lived code in response to events. They are useful for bursty event processing, API handlers, automation, and glue logic when limits and retry behavior are designed explicitly.

nodes.label: Linux Command Line Essentials
nodes.excerpt: Linux command line essentials cover navigating files, inspecting processes, managing text streams, checking resources, editing permissions, and debugging services. Fluency at the shell is still one of the highest-leverage infrastructure skills.

nodes.label: Log Aggregation (ELK Stack, Loki)
nodes.excerpt: Log aggregation centralizes application and infrastructure logs so engineers can search, correlate, alert, and debug across machines and services. ELK and Loki represent two common approaches to storing and querying logs.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Memory Management in Operating Systems
nodes.label: Memory Management in Operating Systems
nodes.excerpt: Operating-system memory management gives processes isolated virtual address spaces backed by physical memory, page tables, caches, and swap. Understanding pages, page faults, OOM behavior, and memory pressure helps debug production systems.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Observability — Logs, Metrics, Traces (The Three Pillars)
nodes.label: Observability — Logs, Metrics, Traces (The Three Pillars)
nodes.excerpt: Observability uses logs, metrics, and traces to understand system behavior from the outside. The goal is not collecting more telemetry, but making production failures diagnosable and service health measurable.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/On-Call Culture and Incident Response
nodes.label: On-Call Culture and Incident Response
nodes.excerpt: On-call is the human operating layer for production systems. Healthy on-call has clear severity levels (P0–P3), defined roles (IC + comms lead + ops), fast alert-to-ack SLAs, a blameless culture, and measured toil. Unhealthy on-call is characterized by alert fatigue, absent runbooks, no incident commander role, and engineers too burned out to be effective. DORA research: on-call load is one of the top predictors of engineering team burnout.

nodes.label: OpenTelemetry
nodes.excerpt: OpenTelemetry is an open standard and toolkit for collecting traces, metrics, and logs across services. It helps teams avoid vendor-specific instrumentation while preserving context propagation and consistent telemetry.

nodes.label: Performance Profiling (CPU, Memory, I-O)
nodes.excerpt: Performance profiling identifies where CPU, memory, I/O, network, or lock contention is limiting a system. Good profiling starts from symptoms and measurements before optimizing code or infrastructure.

nodes.label: Postmortem Writing
nodes.excerpt: A postmortem turns an incident into organizational learning. Structure: impact summary, timeline, contributing factors (not a single root cause), detection/response quality assessment, and concrete action items with owners and due dates. Blameless means the individual made a reasonable decision given their context — the system allowed a bad outcome. Good action items change the system; bad ones say 'be more careful.

nodes.label: Prometheus and Grafana
nodes.excerpt: Prometheus collects time-series metrics and Grafana visualizes them in dashboards and alerts. Together they are a common open-source stack for monitoring service health, latency, errors, saturation, and infrastructure behavior.

nodes.label: Reverse Proxies (Nginx, Caddy, Traefik)
nodes.excerpt: Reverse proxies sit in front of backend services to route traffic, terminate TLS, balance load, enforce policies, and add observability. Nginx, Caddy, and Traefik represent different tradeoffs in control, automation, and dynamic configuration.

nodes.label: Runbooks and Playbooks
nodes.excerpt: Runbooks are step-by-step operational procedures for specific symptoms or alerts. Playbooks are broader scenario flows (e.g., database failover, regional outage). Both reduce cognitive load during incidents by replacing improvisation with verified steps. A runbook is good when a junior engineer can follow it at 2am without asking for help. The main failure mode: stale runbooks that haven't been tested since they were written.

nodes.label: SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs
nodes.excerpt: SLIs measure service behavior, SLOs set reliability targets, and SLAs define external commitments and consequences. Together they translate reliability from vague uptime goals into measurable engineering decisions.

nodes.label: SSH Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: SSH best practices reduce the risk of unauthorized server access while preserving secure administrative workflows. Key ideas include key-based auth, least privilege, bastions, agent hygiene, host verification, logging, and avoiding shared credentials.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Secret Management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
nodes.label: Secret Management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
nodes.excerpt: Secret management stores, distributes, rotates, and audits credentials such as API keys, database passwords, certificates, and tokens. Tools like Vault and AWS Secrets Manager reduce hardcoded secrets but still require careful IAM, rotation, and runtime access design.

nodes.label: Serverless Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Serverless architecture uses managed services where the provider handles server provisioning, scaling, and much of the operations surface. It can reduce infrastructure toil, but shifts design concerns to events, limits, cold starts, observability, cost shape, and vendor boundaries.

nodes.label: Terraform Basics
nodes.excerpt: Terraform is a declarative Infrastructure as Code tool that plans and applies changes to cloud and platform resources. Its core model is providers, resources, modules, state, variables, outputs, and plan/apply workflows.

nodes.label: VPN Architecture
nodes.excerpt: VPN architecture creates encrypted network connectivity between users, devices, offices, clouds, or private networks. Good VPN design considers identity, routing, split tunneling, access scope, device trust, performance, and whether zero-trust access is a better fit.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/systemd — Service and Process Management
nodes.label: systemd — Service and Process Management
nodes.excerpt: systemd manages Linux services, process supervision, dependencies, logs, timers, and boot-time units. Understanding unit files, service lifecycle, journald, and restart behavior is essential for operating Linux servers.

nodes.label: Distributed Systems
nodes.excerpt: partial failures, CAP/PACELC, consistency models, replication (single-leader/multi-leader/leaderless), Raft consensus, 2PC vs Saga, vector clocks, gossip protocol.

nodes.label: Docker
nodes.excerpt: OverlayFS layer system, Dockerfile best practices (multi-stage builds), networking (bridge/host), volumes, container runtime architecture (dockerd→containerd→runc→kernel).

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: focused DP guide covering state design, memoization vs tabulation, 1D/2D DP, knapsack, LIS, interval DP, tree DP, bitmask DP, common traps, and practice method.

nodes.label: Behavioral Economics Overview
nodes.excerpt: Behavioral economics studies how real human judgment departs from perfectly rational models. It combines economics, psychology, bounded rationality, heuristics, biases, defaults, framing, and incentives.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Bitcoin — Blockchain, Proof of Work, Mining
nodes.label: Bitcoin — Blockchain, Proof of Work, Mining
nodes.excerpt: Bitcoin is a decentralized digital asset system using a blockchain, proof-of-work consensus, mining incentives, cryptographic signatures, and a fixed issuance schedule. Its design trades throughput and energy use for censorship resistance and settlement assurance.

nodes.label: Cryptocurrency — Technical Overview
nodes.excerpt: Cryptocurrency uses cryptography, distributed ledgers, consensus mechanisms, wallets, and incentives to coordinate digital assets without a traditional central operator. Technical novelty does not remove economic, security, governance, or regulatory risk.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/DeFi Basics (Protocols, Liquidity Pools, Yield)
nodes.label: DeFi Basics (Protocols, Liquidity Pools, Yield)
nodes.excerpt: Decentralized finance uses smart contracts to provide trading, lending, borrowing, derivatives, stablecoins, and liquidity pools. It is programmable finance, but smart-contract, oracle, governance, leverage, and liquidity risks are central.

nodes.label: Externalities and Market Failure
nodes.excerpt: Externalities occur when market actions impose costs or benefits on third parties not reflected in prices. They are a major reason markets can fail to produce socially efficient outcomes.

nodes.label: GDP and Economic Indicators
nodes.excerpt: GDP measures the market value of final goods and services produced in an economy, but it is only one indicator. Inflation, unemployment, productivity, wages, debt, trade, inequality, and sentiment all add context.

nodes.label: Game Theory Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Game theory studies strategic situations where each participant's outcome depends on what others do. It is useful for understanding incentives, coordination, competition, bargaining, signaling, and repeated interaction.

nodes.label: Globalization — Benefits and Costs
nodes.excerpt: Globalization integrates economies through trade, capital, migration, supply chains, technology, and institutions. It can raise efficiency and growth while also creating disruption, dependency, inequality, and political backlash.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Inflation — Types, Causes, and Effects
nodes.label: Inflation — Types, Causes, and Effects
nodes.excerpt: Inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level, reducing purchasing power. It can arise from demand pressure, supply shocks, expectations, monetary conditions, and sector-specific bottlenecks.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Interest Rates and Central Banks (The Fed, ECB)
nodes.label: Interest Rates and Central Banks (The Fed, ECB)
nodes.excerpt: Central banks influence financial conditions through interest rates, balance sheets, liquidity tools, and communication. The Fed and ECB use monetary policy to pursue mandates around price stability, employment, and broader economic stability.

nodes.label: Microeconomics vs Macroeconomics
nodes.excerpt: Microeconomics studies individual markets, firms, consumers, incentives, and prices, while macroeconomics studies aggregate output, inflation, unemployment, growth, and policy. The two levels connect but use different abstractions.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Monetary Policy vs Fiscal Policy
nodes.label: Monetary Policy vs Fiscal Policy
nodes.excerpt: Monetary policy is conducted by central banks through rates, liquidity, and financial conditions, while fiscal policy is conducted by governments through taxation, spending, transfers, and borrowing. They interact but have different tools, timelines, and political constraints.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/NFTs — What They Are and Why They Matter (or Don't)
nodes.label: NFTs — What They Are and Why They Matter (or Don't)
nodes.excerpt: NFTs are blockchain tokens intended to represent unique assets, claims, or records. Their importance depends on use case: provenance and digital coordination can matter, but ownership, rights, speculation, liquidity, and permanence are often misunderstood.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Network Effects in Economics (Metcalfe's Law)
nodes.label: Network Effects in Economics (Metcalfe's Law)
nodes.excerpt: Network effects occur when a product or platform becomes more valuable as more people use it. They can create strong defensibility, winner-take-most markets, switching costs, and platform power.

nodes.label: Platform Economics (Multi-Sided Markets)
nodes.excerpt: Platform economics studies businesses that coordinate multiple groups, such as buyers and sellers, riders and drivers, or creators and audiences. Value depends on pricing, trust, liquidity, governance, and cross-side network effects.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Prisoner's Dilemma and Nash Equilibrium
nodes.label: Prisoner's Dilemma and Nash Equilibrium
nodes.excerpt: The Prisoner's Dilemma shows how individually rational choices can produce collectively worse outcomes. Nash equilibrium captures a stable strategy profile where no player wants to change alone.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Public Goods and the Free Rider Problem
nodes.label: Public Goods and the Free Rider Problem
nodes.excerpt: Public goods are non-rival and non-excludable, meaning one person's use does not reduce another's and people cannot easily be excluded. The free rider problem makes voluntary funding difficult.

nodes.label: Rent-Seeking vs Wealth Creation
nodes.excerpt: Wealth creation increases real value, productivity, usefulness, or capacity, while rent-seeking captures value through control, privilege, scarcity, regulation, or gatekeeping. Distinguishing them clarifies economic and ethical arguments.

nodes.label: Supply and Demand Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Supply and demand explain how prices and quantities emerge from buyers' willingness to pay and sellers' willingness to produce. The framework is simple but powerful when paired with elasticity, market structure, and real-world frictions.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Technology and Labor Markets (Automation and Jobs)
nodes.label: Technology and Labor Markets (Automation and Jobs)
nodes.excerpt: Technology changes labor markets by automating tasks, complementing workers, creating new jobs, shifting bargaining power, and changing skill demand. The effects depend on institutions, timing, diffusion, and who captures productivity gains.

nodes.label: The Economics of Attention
nodes.excerpt: The economics of attention treats human attention as a scarce resource competed for by media, platforms, advertisers, creators, and products. Business models shape incentives around engagement, interruption, personalization, and habit formation.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Universal Basic Income — Arguments For and Against
nodes.label: Universal Basic Income — Arguments For and Against
nodes.excerpt: Universal Basic Income is a recurring cash payment to all members of a population, usually without means testing or work requirements. Arguments center on poverty, autonomy, bureaucracy, labor incentives, inflation, cost, and political feasibility.

nodes.label: Winner-Take-All Markets
nodes.excerpt: Winner-take-all and winner-take-most markets concentrate rewards among a small number of firms or individuals. They often arise from network effects, scale economies, distribution advantages, superstar dynamics, or platform control.

nodes.label: Economics and Personal Finance
nodes.excerpt: practical economics and personal finance primer covering incentives, opportunity cost, supply/demand, compounding, emergency funds, debt, index funds, retirement accounts, taxes, credit, lifestyle inflation, and career finance.

nodes.label: Engineering Judgment and Tool Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: practical guide to choosing tools by fit, failure modes, accepted tradeoffs, monitoring signals, and simpler alternatives.

nodes.label: Building in Public
nodes.excerpt: Building in public means sharing progress, decisions, lessons, metrics, and failures while creating a product or business. It can create trust and distribution, but it also risks performative work, copycats, and audience distraction.

nodes.label: Cap Table Basics
nodes.excerpt: A cap table records who owns a company, including founders, employees, investors, options, convertible instruments, and share classes. It is the source of truth for ownership, dilution, and financing scenarios.

nodes.label: Community-Led Growth
nodes.excerpt: Community-led growth uses shared identity, trust, peer learning, and participation to support acquisition, retention, feedback, and advocacy. It works when community members receive real value beyond being marketed to.

nodes.label: Customer Discovery
nodes.excerpt: Customer discovery is the process of learning whether a real customer segment has a painful problem, current alternatives, buying motivation, and budget. The goal is evidence, not compliments.

nodes.label: Distribution is Everything
nodes.excerpt: Distribution is the ability to reach, persuade, sell to, and retain the right customers. A strong product without a path to customers can fail, while strong distribution can compound product learning and market power.

nodes.label: Equity and Dilution
nodes.excerpt: Equity represents ownership in a company, while dilution reduces an owner's percentage when new shares are issued. Dilution can be worthwhile when new capital or talent increases the company's total value enough.

nodes.label: Finding Product-Market Fit
nodes.excerpt: Product-market fit happens when a product satisfies a strong market need well enough that customers pull it forward. It is visible through retention, usage, referrals, urgency, willingness to pay, and painful disappointment if the product disappears.

nodes.label: Founder-Market Fit
nodes.excerpt: Founder-market fit is the alignment between a founder's insight, credibility, relationships, motivation, and market needs. It increases the odds of finding useful problems, earning trust, and persisting through ambiguity.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/How VC Funds Work (Management Fees, Carry, Fund Size)
nodes.label: How VC Funds Work (Management Fees, Carry, Fund Size)
nodes.excerpt: Venture capital funds raise money from limited partners, invest through general partners, charge management fees, and earn carried interest on gains. Fund size and power-law returns shape investor behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Jobs to Be Done Framework
nodes.label: Jobs to Be Done Framework
nodes.excerpt: Jobs to Be Done frames customers as hiring products to make progress in a specific situation. It focuses on context, desired outcomes, switching triggers, anxieties, and competing alternatives.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/MVP Design — What Minimum Actually Means
nodes.label: MVP Design — What Minimum Actually Means
nodes.excerpt: An MVP is the smallest experiment that can test a meaningful product or business assumption. Minimum means minimum learning vehicle, not a low-quality version of the final product.

nodes.label: Pricing Strategy (Value-Based, Cost-Plus, Freemium)
nodes.excerpt: Pricing strategy determines how a business captures value from what it creates. Common approaches include value-based pricing, cost-plus pricing, competitive pricing, usage pricing, subscriptions, tiers, and freemium.

nodes.label: Solopreneurship and Indie Hacking
nodes.excerpt: Solopreneurship and indie hacking focus on building small, profitable, often software-enabled businesses without needing venture-scale growth. The emphasis is autonomy, distribution, low overhead, and direct customer revenue.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Startup Funding (Bootstrapping, Angel, Pre-Seed, Seed, Series A)
nodes.label: Startup Funding (Bootstrapping, Angel, Pre-Seed, Seed, Series A)
nodes.excerpt: Startup funding ranges from bootstrapping and customer revenue to angel checks, pre-seed, seed, and Series A venture rounds. Each path changes expectations around speed, ownership, control, risk, and exit size.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Startup vs Small Business — Key Differences
nodes.label: Startup vs Small Business — Key Differences
nodes.excerpt: A startup is designed to search for a scalable, repeatable business model under uncertainty, while a small business usually executes a more known model in a defined market. The difference is growth ambition, uncertainty, financing, and operating style.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Term Sheets — Key Terms to Negotiate
nodes.label: Term Sheets — Key Terms to Negotiate
nodes.excerpt: A term sheet outlines the economic and control terms of a startup financing. Key terms include valuation, investment amount, liquidation preference, pro rata rights, board control, option pool, vesting, and protective provisions.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/The Art of the Pivot
nodes.label: The Art of the Pivot
nodes.excerpt: A pivot is a strategic change in customer, problem, product, channel, business model, or positioning based on evidence. Good pivots preserve learning while abandoning assumptions that are not working.

nodes.label: The Lean Startup Method (Build-Measure-Learn)
nodes.excerpt: The Lean Startup method treats startups as experiments that move through build-measure-learn loops. The goal is validated learning: discovering whether assumptions about customers, problems, solutions, and business models are true.

nodes.label: Timing a Market
nodes.excerpt: Market timing is the fit between a product idea and the external conditions that make adoption possible. Technology readiness, customer pain, regulation, budgets, behavior, and distribution channels can make the same idea too early, too late, or right on time.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Unit Economics (CAC, LTV, Payback Period, Churn)
nodes.label: Unit Economics (CAC, LTV, Payback Period, Churn)
nodes.excerpt: Unit economics measure whether acquiring and serving a customer creates durable profit. Core metrics include CAC, LTV, gross margin, payback period, churn, retention, expansion, and contribution margin.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Y Combinator — Lessons and Application Advice
nodes.label: Y Combinator — Lessons and Application Advice
nodes.excerpt: Y Combinator is a startup accelerator and investor known for advice such as make something people want, talk to users, launch quickly, and focus on growth. Strong applications are clear, specific, evidence-based, and founder-market aware.

nodes.label: Character Arc Design
nodes.excerpt: Character arc design tracks how a character changes or resists change through desire, flaw, pressure, choice, consequence, and revelation. Strong arcs connect external plot to internal transformation.

nodes.label: Cinematography Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Cinematography uses camera placement, movement, lens choice, framing, lighting, exposure, color, and composition to shape how a story is seen and felt. It translates narrative meaning into visual experience.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/Editing Principles (Continuity, Montage, Jump Cut)
nodes.label: Editing Principles (Continuity, Montage, Jump Cut)
nodes.excerpt: Film editing shapes time, rhythm, attention, emotion, and meaning by arranging shots. Continuity, montage, and jump cuts represent different ways to preserve, compress, or disrupt the viewer's experience.

nodes.label: Genre Conventions
nodes.excerpt: Genre conventions are recurring expectations around plot, tone, character, setting, conflict, pacing, and payoff. They help audiences orient, but strong stories often satisfy and subvert conventions deliberately.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/Narrative Structure (3-Act, Hero's Journey, Story Circle, Save the Cat)
nodes.label: Narrative Structure (3-Act, Hero's Journey, Story Circle, Save the Cat)
nodes.excerpt: Narrative structure frameworks organize stories through setup, escalation, transformation, crisis, and resolution. Models like 3-act structure, Hero's Journey, Story Circle, and Save the Cat are tools, not formulas.

nodes.label: Screenplay Writing Basics
nodes.excerpt: Screenplay writing tells stories through visible action, dialogue, scene structure, pacing, and cinematic implication. A screenplay is a blueprint for a film, not a novel in script format.

nodes.label: The Pixar Storytelling Formula
nodes.excerpt: The Pixar storytelling formula is a compact story scaffold: once there was, every day, until one day, because of that, until finally. It emphasizes disruption, causality, escalation, and resolution.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/The Role of Conflict in Story
nodes.label: The Role of Conflict in Story
nodes.excerpt: Conflict is the pressure that forces characters to reveal values, make choices, and change. It can be external, internal, interpersonal, social, moral, or existential, and it gives story its forward motion.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/Why Certain Stories Resonate (Evolutionary Psychology of Narrative)
nodes.label: Why Certain Stories Resonate (Evolutionary Psychology of Narrative)
nodes.excerpt: Certain stories resonate because they engage social learning, threat detection, status, love, betrayal, belonging, morality, identity, and transformation. Evolutionary psychology can offer hypotheses, but should not reduce art to one adaptive function.

nodes.label: Worldbuilding Principles
nodes.excerpt: Worldbuilding creates coherent settings through history, geography, culture, power, technology, economy, ecology, constraints, and lived detail. Strong worlds feel bigger than the plot but still serve the story.

nodes.label: FlashAttention
nodes.excerpt: GPU memory hierarchy, why standard attention is HBM-bound, tiling algorithm, online softmax, FA-2 and FA-3.

nodes.label: Formal Methods Agent Safety
nodes.excerpt: temporal logic specs, type systems, runtime monitors, NN verification (and its limits), probabilistic verification, neurosymbolic approaches.

nodes.label: Competitive Gaming Psychology
nodes.excerpt: Competitive gaming psychology studies performance under pressure, attention, tilt, confidence, motivation, teamwork, practice, and decision-making. Mental skill can decide outcomes when mechanical skill is similar.

nodes.label: Difficulty Scaling in Games
nodes.excerpt: Difficulty scaling adjusts challenge over time through enemy behavior, resource pressure, level design, progression, adaptive systems, and optional mastery. Good scaling preserves tension without feeling unfair or patronizing.

nodes.label: Esports Industry Overview
nodes.excerpt: Esports is the organized competitive gaming ecosystem of players, teams, leagues, publishers, sponsors, broadcasters, platforms, tournaments, and fans. Its economics depend heavily on game ownership, media rights, sponsorship, and community engagement.

nodes.label: Game Balance Design
nodes.excerpt: Game balance tunes systems so choices, challenges, rewards, and strategies feel fair, interesting, and sustainable. Balance is contextual: perfect symmetry is not always the goal.

nodes.label: Game Design Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Game design creates structured play through goals, rules, feedback, challenge, choice, progression, and reward. Good design aligns mechanics, dynamics, aesthetics, and player motivation.

nodes.label: Game Narrative Design
nodes.excerpt: Game narrative design integrates story with mechanics, player agency, world, pacing, dialogue, quests, and environmental storytelling. The challenge is making story respond to play rather than merely interrupt it.

nodes.label: Game Physics Engines Overview
nodes.excerpt: Game physics engines simulate motion, collision, forces, constraints, rigid bodies, and sometimes soft bodies or fluids. They prioritize believable, stable, performant behavior over perfect physical realism.

nodes.label: Gaming and Flow State
nodes.excerpt: Gaming can produce flow when challenge and skill are well matched, goals are clear, feedback is immediate, and attention is fully absorbed. Flow explains why well-designed games feel compelling and time-distorting.

nodes.id: Resources/Gaming/Monetization Models in Games (Gacha, Battle Pass, DLC)
nodes.label: Monetization Models in Games (Gacha, Battle Pass, DLC)
nodes.excerpt: Game monetization models include premium purchase, DLC, subscriptions, cosmetics, battle passes, ads, loot boxes, and gacha systems. Monetization shapes design incentives and can conflict with player trust.

nodes.label: Open World Design
nodes.excerpt: Open world design creates large navigable spaces with exploration, landmarks, traversal, activities, pacing, systemic interaction, and player-driven goals. The core challenge is making freedom meaningful rather than empty.

nodes.label: Pathfinding Algorithms (A*, NavMesh)
nodes.excerpt: Pathfinding algorithms help game agents move through environments using graph search, heuristics, navigation meshes, and local avoidance. A* and NavMesh systems balance correctness, realism, and performance.

nodes.label: Procedural Generation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Procedural generation creates game content algorithmically, including levels, terrain, items, quests, names, maps, and encounters. Good procedural systems combine randomness, constraints, authored rules, and quality control.

nodes.label: The Game Loop (Input, Update, Render)
nodes.excerpt: The game loop repeatedly processes input, updates simulation state, and renders output. It is the runtime heartbeat that turns code, assets, and rules into continuous interactive experience.

nodes.id: Resources/Gaming/The Psychology of Loot Boxes and Variable Rewards
nodes.label: The Psychology of Loot Boxes and Variable Rewards
nodes.excerpt: Loot boxes and variable rewards use uncertainty, anticipation, scarcity, loss aversion, and social comparison to increase engagement and spending. They raise design, ethical, and regulatory concerns when they exploit vulnerable players.

nodes.label: Geospatial
nodes.excerpt: geohashing (8-neighbor fix), quadtrees, S2/Hilbert curve, Haversine formula, Yelp-like proximity design, Uber-like live driver matching with Redis GEOSEARCH, H3 hexagons, map tile systems.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/ARM Architecture and the Embedded Computing Ecosystem
nodes.label: ARM Architecture and the Embedded Computing Ecosystem
nodes.excerpt: ARM is a RISC ISA licensed by Arm Holdings, powering virtually all mobile devices and a large portion of embedded systems worldwide. ARM's business model is licensing IP rather than making chips, which means dozens of companies (Apple, Qualcomm, Samsung, NVIDIA) implement the ISA with wildly different microarchitectures. Understanding ARM explains the mobile performance revolution and Apple Silicon's dominance.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/ASICs vs FPGAs vs CPUs — Design Tradeoffs and When to Use Each
nodes.label: ASICs vs FPGAs vs CPUs — Design Tradeoffs and When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: CPUs are general-purpose and flexible; FPGAs are reconfigurable hardware that can implement custom logic; ASICs are fixed-function chips optimized for exactly one task. The tradeoffs are cost, performance, power, development time, and volume — understanding the decision matrix explains why Bitcoin mines use ASICs, why network switches run ASICs, why ML inference is moving from GPUs to NPUs, and why FPGAs are used in data centers.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Arduino vs Raspberry Pi — Architecture, Use Cases, and What to Pick
nodes.label: Arduino vs Raspberry Pi — Architecture, Use Cases, and What to Pick
nodes.excerpt: Arduino is a microcontroller platform — it boots directly into your code with no OS, millisecond boot time, and μA sleep modes. Raspberry Pi is a Linux single-board computer with GHz CPU, gigabytes of RAM, and a full OS. They're not competing products — they solve different problems, and the right choice is usually obvious once you know which model each represents.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/CPU Architecture (RISC vs CISC, ARM vs x86)
nodes.label: CPU Architecture (RISC vs CISC, ARM vs x86)
nodes.excerpt: CPU architecture defines the instruction set and execution design exposed to software, with RISC emphasizing simpler fixed-style instructions and CISC emphasizing richer instructions. Modern ARM and x86 chips blur the old divide internally, but the distinction still helps explain compatibility, power efficiency, and ecosystem tradeoffs.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/CPU Microarchitecture — Pipelines, Branch Prediction, Out-of-Order Execution
nodes.label: CPU Microarchitecture — Pipelines, Branch Prediction, Out-of-Order Execution
nodes.excerpt: CPU microarchitecture defines how instructions are executed inside a processor. Pipelines allow overlapping stages of multiple instructions; out-of-order execution reorders independent instructions to fill execution units; branch prediction speculatively executes future instructions before knowing if a branch is taken. These techniques explain most of modern CPU performance — and its vulnerabilities.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Chip Packaging Technologies (BGA, Flip-Chip, Chiplets, 2.5D-3D Stacking)
nodes.label: Chip Packaging Technologies (BGA, Flip-Chip, Chiplets, 2.5D-3D Stacking)
nodes.excerpt: Chip packaging connects a bare die to the outside world. BGA (Ball Grid Array) is the standard PCB-mountable package. Flip-chip reverses the die orientation so bumps connect directly to the substrate, reducing parasitics. Chiplets decompose a large monolithic die into smaller dies assembled in one package using 2.5D (silicon interposer) or 3D (die stacking with TSVs) integration. These advanced packaging techniques are now the primary path for scaling performance when transistor density improvements alone aren't enough.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Clock Signals, Crystal Oscillators, PLLs, and Timing
nodes.label: Clock Signals, Crystal Oscillators, PLLs, and Timing
nodes.excerpt: Digital systems are synchronous machines driven by clock signals. Crystal oscillators provide accurate, stable frequencies from piezoelectric resonance. PLLs (Phase-Locked Loops) multiply and divide that reference to generate the many clock frequencies a SoC needs. Clock quality — frequency accuracy, jitter, skew — directly affects whether a system's timing margins hold or fail.

nodes.label: DRAM vs SRAM
nodes.excerpt: DRAM and SRAM are volatile memory technologies with different tradeoffs: DRAM is dense and cheap enough for main memory, while SRAM is faster and more expensive, making it suitable for CPU caches. The distinction explains why computers have separate RAM and cache layers instead of one universal memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Digital Logic — Gates, Flip-Flops, Registers, and Finite State Machines
nodes.label: Digital Logic — Gates, Flip-Flops, Registers, and Finite State Machines
nodes.excerpt: Digital logic is the foundation of all computing hardware: Boolean gates implement logic functions, flip-flops store a single bit of state synchronized to a clock, registers group flip-flops to store a word, and finite state machines combine registers with combinational logic to implement any sequential behavior. Everything from a CPU to a USB controller is built from these primitives.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/DisplayPort vs HDMI vs Thunderbolt
nodes.label: DisplayPort vs HDMI vs Thunderbolt
nodes.excerpt: DisplayPort, HDMI, and Thunderbolt all carry video in different ecosystems: HDMI dominates TVs and consoles, DisplayPort is common for PCs and high-refresh monitors, and Thunderbolt carries video plus high-speed data over USB-C. Choosing between them depends on resolution, refresh rate, VRR, HDR, docking, cable quality, and device support.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) — Sources, Shielding, and FCC Certification
nodes.label: Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) — Sources, Shielding, and FCC Certification
nodes.excerpt: EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) is unwanted electromagnetic energy that disrupts other devices. Every digital circuit emits EMI — clocks, switching regulators, and fast edges are dominant sources. FCC Part 15 / CE certification sets legal limits on emissions before a product can be sold. EMI mitigation starts in PCB layout and schematic, and is tested in an EMC chamber. Failing an EMC test is expensive: $5–20K for a test session and weeks of redesign.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Electronic Testing Tools — Multimeter, Oscilloscope, Logic Analyzer, Spectrum Analyzer
nodes.label: Electronic Testing Tools — Multimeter, Oscilloscope, Logic Analyzer, Spectrum Analyzer
nodes.excerpt: Electronic test tools are how you observe what a circuit is actually doing, not what you think it should be doing. A multimeter measures DC voltage, current, and resistance. An oscilloscope shows signal waveforms over time. A logic analyzer captures digital protocol data. A spectrum analyzer shows frequency-domain power distribution. Each tool answers a different class of question.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/FPGAs — Reprogrammable Hardware and How HDL Compiles to Gates
nodes.label: FPGAs — Reprogrammable Hardware and How HDL Compiles to Gates
nodes.excerpt: FPGAs are integrated circuits with programmable logic fabric — millions of configurable look-up tables, flip-flops, DSP blocks, and memory that can be wired together to implement any digital circuit. HDL (Verilog/VHDL) is synthesized into a netlist, then placed and routed onto the FPGA's programmable fabric via bitstream. FPGAs sit between CPUs (flexible, slow) and ASICs (fast, fixed) — chosen when you need custom hardware logic without the cost and time of a chip tape-out.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/GPIO and Embedded Interfaces (I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, PWM)
nodes.label: GPIO and Embedded Interfaces (I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, PWM)
nodes.excerpt: GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, and PWM are the basic communication and control interfaces on every microcontroller and SBC. Each protocol has distinct speed, wiring, and addressing tradeoffs. Knowing which protocol a sensor or peripheral uses — and why — is the core skill for embedded hardware selection and debugging.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/HDMI and DisplayPort Signal Standards — How Video Is Transmitted Digitally
nodes.label: HDMI and DisplayPort Signal Standards — How Video Is Transmitted Digitally
nodes.excerpt: HDMI and DisplayPort are the two dominant digital video standards. HDMI is consumer-focused, carries video + audio + CEC over a single cable, and is royalty-bearing. DisplayPort is industry/PC-focused, royalty-free, and carries video at higher bandwidth with more flexible multi-stream support. Both transmit pixel data as high-speed differential serial streams. USB-C with DisplayPort Alt Mode carries DP signal over the USB-C physical layer, enabling a single cable for power + data + video.

nodes.label: Home Lab Setup Guide
nodes.excerpt: A home lab is a small personal infrastructure environment for learning networking, servers, storage, containers, automation, and observability. A good setup starts with clear goals, modest hardware, backups, network segmentation, documentation, and services that are useful enough to maintain.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How CPUs Work (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Write-Back)
nodes.label: How CPUs Work (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Write-Back)
nodes.excerpt: CPUs execute programs through an instruction cycle: fetch an instruction, decode it, execute the operation, and write results back to registers or memory. This simple model explains the baseline, while modern CPUs add pipelining, caches, branch prediction, and out-of-order execution to keep work flowing.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How GPUs Work and Why ML Uses Them
nodes.label: How GPUs Work and Why ML Uses Them
nodes.excerpt: GPUs are built for massive parallel throughput: many smaller cores execute similar operations across large batches of data. Machine learning uses them because neural network training and inference are dominated by matrix operations that map well to GPU parallelism and high memory bandwidth.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How NAND Flash Works (SLC-MLC-TLC-QLC Cells, Wear Leveling, FTL Controllers)
nodes.label: How NAND Flash Works (SLC-MLC-TLC-QLC Cells, Wear Leveling, FTL Controllers)
nodes.excerpt: NAND flash stores data in floating-gate transistors by trapping charge. SLC stores 1 bit per cell with long endurance; TLC stores 3 bits with lower endurance but much higher density and lower cost. An FTL (Flash Translation Layer) in the SSD controller handles wear leveling, garbage collection, and the logical-to-physical address mapping that hides NAND's erase-before-write constraint from the OS. These mechanisms explain write amplification, endurance ratings (TBW), and why SSDs slow down under sustained writes.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How NPUs Work — Neural Processing Units in Modern SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon)
nodes.label: How NPUs Work — Neural Processing Units in Modern SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon)
nodes.excerpt: NPUs (Neural Processing Units) are fixed-function hardware accelerators for matrix multiply and activation operations — the core operations of neural network inference. They outperform CPUs and GPUs by 10–100× per watt for supported model types because they eliminate the general-purpose overhead of both. Apple's Neural Engine, Qualcomm Hexagon NPU, and Google's Edge TPU are the dominant mobile implementations. Understanding NPUs explains on-device AI performance claims and which operations actually use them.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How Semiconductors Work (Silicon, Doping, P-N Junctions, Transistors)
nodes.label: How Semiconductors Work (Silicon, Doping, P-N Junctions, Transistors)
nodes.excerpt: Semiconductors are materials whose conductivity can be precisely controlled by doping and electric fields. Silicon transistors — billions per chip — are the physical switches that make all digital computation possible. Understanding them explains why chips run hot, why process nodes matter, and what limits clock speeds.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How USB Works Internally (Host Controller, Enumeration, Transfer Types)
nodes.label: How USB Works Internally (Host Controller, Enumeration, Transfer Types)
nodes.excerpt: USB is a host-centric, tiered serial bus. The host controller (EHCI, xHCI) manages all device communication — devices can't initiate transactions. On plug-in, the host enumerates the device: assigns address, reads descriptors, loads driver. USB supports four transfer types with different bandwidth/latency guarantees. Understanding enumeration and transfer types explains USB device class drivers, why some USB hubs cause issues, and how USB audio/video latency works.
