nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How a CPU is Fabricated (Photolithography, EUV, TSMC vs Intel Fabs, Yield)
nodes.label: How a CPU is Fabricated (Photolithography, EUV, TSMC vs Intel Fabs, Yield)
nodes.excerpt: CPU fabrication uses photolithography to pattern billions of transistors onto silicon wafers. TSMC, Intel, and Samsung compete at 3nm and below using EUV light sources. Yield — the fraction of good dies per wafer — determines cost and explains why cutting-edge chips are expensive and take time to ramp.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How a GPU is Designed (Shader Cores, SIMD Lanes, Tensor Cores vs CPU Architecture)
nodes.label: How a GPU is Designed (Shader Cores, SIMD Lanes, Tensor Cores vs CPU Architecture)
nodes.excerpt: GPUs trade deep per-thread pipelines for thousands of simple shader cores executing the same operation across massive datasets in parallel. This SIMD/SIMT design makes them ideal for graphics, ML training, and simulation — but poor for branchy, latency-sensitive code. Tensor Cores (matrix multiply accelerators) are the key hardware reason why modern AI training happens on GPUs.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Memory Hierarchy (L1-L2-L3 Cache, RAM, Disk)
nodes.label: Memory Hierarchy (L1-L2-L3 Cache, RAM, Disk)
nodes.excerpt: The memory hierarchy arranges storage by speed, size, and cost: registers and caches are tiny and fast, RAM is larger and slower, and disks are much larger and slower still. Performance often depends on keeping hot data close to the CPU and avoiding unnecessary movement through the hierarchy.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Memory Technologies — LPDDR vs DDR5 vs HBM and Why It Matters for AI
nodes.label: Memory Technologies — LPDDR vs DDR5 vs HBM and Why It Matters for AI
nodes.excerpt: DRAM memory comes in multiple packaging and interface variants optimized for different compute contexts. DDR5 is the current desktop/server standard. LPDDR5X is the mobile variant with lower power but narrower bus. HBM3 is 3D-stacked DRAM directly bonded to the GPU die — delivering 10–20× the bandwidth of DDR5 at a fraction of the energy per bit. HBM bandwidth is why LLM inference throughput scales with memory bandwidth, not compute.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Microcontrollers vs Microprocessors — Where Each Belongs
nodes.label: Microcontrollers vs Microprocessors — Where Each Belongs
nodes.excerpt: A microcontroller (MCU) integrates CPU, RAM, flash, and peripherals on one chip — it boots and runs directly, with no OS required. A microprocessor (MPU) is a CPU that needs external RAM, storage, and peripherals, typically running a full OS like Linux. The choice drives the entire development stack: bare-metal C vs Linux userspace, interrupt-driven vs process-based, μA sleep modes vs GHz throughput.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Motherboard and Bus Architecture (PCIe, DMI)
nodes.label: Motherboard and Bus Architecture (PCIe, DMI)
nodes.excerpt: A motherboard connects CPU, memory, storage, expansion cards, firmware, power delivery, and I/O through buses and chipset links. PCIe lanes, chipset bandwidth, VRMs, and physical slots determine what hardware can be installed and how much throughput it can actually use.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/NAS and Home Storage (ZFS, RAID Levels)
nodes.label: NAS and Home Storage (ZFS, RAID Levels)
nodes.excerpt: A NAS centralizes storage on the network, often using RAID or ZFS to improve availability, integrity, and manageability. The crucial caveat is that RAID is not backup: it can survive some drive failures, but it does not protect against deletion, corruption, theft, fire, or ransomware by itself.

nodes.label: Networking Hardware (Routers, Switches, NICs)
nodes.excerpt: Networking hardware moves packets between devices and networks: NICs connect hosts, switches connect local devices, and routers connect networks. Understanding these roles helps diagnose home lab, Wi-Fi, latency, throughput, and segmentation problems.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Ohm's Law and Basic Circuit Analysis (KVL, KCL, Voltage Dividers, Thevenin)
nodes.label: Ohm's Law and Basic Circuit Analysis (KVL, KCL, Voltage Dividers, Thevenin)
nodes.excerpt: Ohm's Law (V = IR) and Kirchhoff's Laws are the foundational tools for analyzing any electrical circuit. Voltage dividers, Thévenin equivalents, and nodal analysis are the practical techniques that follow. These let you predict currents, voltages, and power dissipation in any resistive circuit without simulation.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Open Hardware and the Maker Movement (KiCad, OSHW, Raspberry Pi Foundation)
nodes.label: Open Hardware and the Maker Movement (KiCad, OSHW, Raspberry Pi Foundation)
nodes.excerpt: The open hardware movement applies open-source principles to physical electronics: schematics, PCB layouts, and firmware are published under permissive licenses so anyone can study, build, and modify them. KiCad is the dominant open-source EDA tool. OSHW (Open Source Hardware Association) defines the certification standard. The Raspberry Pi Foundation democratized accessible computing hardware. Together they've lowered the barrier for hardware design from 'requires proprietary tools and NDA supplier relationships' to 'anyone with a laptop and $50 for a PCB run.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/PCB Design Basics (Schematic Capture, Layout, Gerbers, Design Rule Checks)
nodes.label: PCB Design Basics (Schematic Capture, Layout, Gerbers, Design Rule Checks)
nodes.excerpt: PCB design turns an electrical schematic into a physical board with copper traces, component pads, and layer stackups. The flow goes: schematic capture → netlist → layout → DRC → Gerber files → fab. Understanding this pipeline explains why hardware bugs are expensive and how board-level signal integrity problems arise.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/PCIe Architecture Deep Dive (Lanes, Gen 4 vs Gen 5, Bifurcation)
nodes.label: PCIe Architecture Deep Dive (Lanes, Gen 4 vs Gen 5, Bifurcation)
nodes.excerpt: PCIe (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) is the universal high-speed bus connecting CPUs to GPUs, NVMe SSDs, NICs, and accelerators. Each lane is a pair of differential serial links; bandwidth scales with lane count and generation. Gen 5 doubles Gen 4 per-lane bandwidth to 32 GT/s, giving a ×16 slot 64 GB/s. Bifurcation, link training, and power delivery are the practical engineering details that affect whether high-speed PCIe works reliably.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Power Management ICs — LDOs vs Buck-Boost Converters
nodes.label: Power Management ICs — LDOs vs Buck-Boost Converters
nodes.excerpt: Power management ICs regulate voltage and current to supply stable, noise-tolerant power to every circuit on a board. LDOs (Low Dropout regulators) are simple, low-noise, but inefficient — they burn excess voltage as heat. Switching regulators (buck, boost, buck-boost) use inductors and switches to convert voltage with 85–95% efficiency but add switching noise. Choosing between them comes down to efficiency, noise, cost, and the voltage conversion ratio needed.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Power Supplies and 80+ Efficiency Ratings
nodes.label: Power Supplies and 80+ Efficiency Ratings
nodes.excerpt: A computer power supply converts wall AC into stable DC rails for components, and 80+ ratings describe efficiency at specified loads. Good PSU selection is about wattage headroom, build quality, protections, connectors, noise, and efficiency rather than chasing the highest advertised number.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/RISC-V — Open Source ISA and Why It Matters
nodes.label: RISC-V — Open Source ISA and Why It Matters
nodes.excerpt: RISC-V is a free and open ISA developed at UC Berkeley, designed for clean modularity and permissive licensing. Unlike ARM, no royalties are required, and the entire specification is public. It's seeing rapid adoption in embedded controllers, custom accelerators, and academic research — and is increasingly competing with ARM in commercial silicon.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Resistors, Capacitors, Inductors — What They Do and How They Behave
nodes.label: Resistors, Capacitors, Inductors — What They Do and How They Behave
nodes.excerpt: Resistors, capacitors, and inductors are the three passive components that all analog and mixed-signal circuits are built from. Resistors dissipate energy as heat, capacitors store energy in an electric field, and inductors store energy in a magnetic field. Their frequency-dependent behavior (impedance) is what makes filters, oscillators, power converters, and matching networks possible.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/SSD vs HDD — How They Work Internally
nodes.label: SSD vs HDD — How They Work Internally
nodes.excerpt: HDDs store data magnetically on spinning platters, while SSDs store data electronically in NAND flash. SSDs are faster for random access and shock resistance, but have write endurance and controller behavior that matter for reliability and performance.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Signal Integrity — Impedance Matching, Transmission Lines, Reflections, Crosstalk
nodes.label: Signal Integrity — Impedance Matching, Transmission Lines, Reflections, Crosstalk
nodes.excerpt: Signal integrity (SI) is the study of how electrical signals behave as they travel through conductors. At high frequencies (above ~100–300MHz), PCB traces behave as transmission lines — not simple wires. Impedance mismatches cause reflections that degrade signal edges; crosstalk causes adjacent traces to interfere; simultaneous switching noise stresses power planes. SI is why high-speed PCB design is a discipline, not just routing.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Single-Board Computers (SBCs) — Key Components and Design (CPU, PMIC, RAM, eMMC)
nodes.label: Single-Board Computers (SBCs) — Key Components and Design (CPU, PMIC, RAM, eMMC)
nodes.excerpt: A single-board computer (SBC) integrates an SoC, DRAM, storage, and I/O onto one PCB — ready to run Linux out of the box. Understanding the components (SoC, PMIC, eMMC, LPDDR) explains performance characteristics, boot failures, thermal limits, and why some SBCs are more reliable than others for production use.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Soldering and PCB Assembly Fundamentals (Through-Hole vs SMD)
nodes.label: Soldering and PCB Assembly Fundamentals (Through-Hole vs SMD)
nodes.excerpt: Soldering is the process of joining metal components with a tin-silver alloy that melts at 183–250°C. Through-hole components use leads passing through drilled holes; SMD components sit on pads on the board surface. Understanding the thermal, mechanical, and process differences between them determines component selection, assembly method, and rework strategies for any electronics project.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Thermal Design and CPU-GPU Cooling
nodes.label: Thermal Design and CPU-GPU Cooling
nodes.excerpt: Thermal design manages heat from CPUs, GPUs, VRMs, storage, and cases so components can sustain performance without throttling or reliability problems. Cooling depends on heat transfer, cooler capacity, airflow, ambient temperature, fan curves, and dust management.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Thermal Design — TDP, Heat Sinks, Heat Pipes, Vapor Chambers, Liquid Cooling
nodes.label: Thermal Design — TDP, Heat Sinks, Heat Pipes, Vapor Chambers, Liquid Cooling
nodes.excerpt: Thermal design is the engineering discipline of moving heat from silicon die to ambient air fast enough that junction temperature stays below the safe limit. TDP defines the expected heat to dissipate. Heat sinks, heat pipes, vapor chambers, and liquid cooling are the progressively more capable technologies for moving that heat. Thermal throttling is the firmware response to exceeding limits — it's what caps CPU/GPU performance in thin laptops, hot rooms, and poorly cooled cases.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/USB Standards Timeline (USB 2.0 through USB4)
nodes.label: USB Standards Timeline (USB 2.0 through USB4)
nodes.excerpt: USB standards evolved from simple peripheral connectivity into high-speed data, charging, display tunneling, and docking. The confusing part is that connector shape, data speed, charging support, and display capability are separate properties that marketing names often blur.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/VLSI Design — From RTL to Tape-Out (Synthesis, Place & Route, Timing Closure)
nodes.label: VLSI Design — From RTL to Tape-Out (Synthesis, Place & Route, Timing Closure)
nodes.excerpt: VLSI design is the process of turning a hardware description (RTL) into a physical chip layout ready for fabrication. Key stages are synthesis (RTL → gate netlist), place & route (gates → physical locations and wire connections), and timing closure (verifying all signals meet clock deadlines). A tape-out is the final delivery of GDS files to the fab.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Back Health for Desk Workers
nodes.label: Back Health for Desk Workers
nodes.excerpt: Back health for desk workers depends on movement variety, strength, load tolerance, sleep, stress, and workstation setup. Sitting is not automatically harmful, but staying rigid and inactive for long periods can be.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Blood Work Markers Worth Tracking
nodes.label: Blood Work Markers Worth Tracking
nodes.excerpt: Blood work can reveal health patterns that subjective feeling misses, but markers need clinical context. Commonly useful areas include lipids, glucose control, blood count, thyroid, liver, kidney, iron, vitamin D, and inflammation markers.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Breathing Techniques (Box Breathing, Physiological Sigh, Wim Hof)
nodes.label: Breathing Techniques (Box Breathing, Physiological Sigh, Wim Hof)
nodes.excerpt: Breathing techniques use deliberate breath patterns to influence arousal, attention, and perceived stress. Box breathing, the physiological sigh, and Wim Hof-style breathing differ in intensity and should be matched to context and safety.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Cardio Types — HIIT vs LISS vs Zone 2
nodes.label: Cardio Types — HIIT vs LISS vs Zone 2
nodes.excerpt: Cardio methods differ by intensity and adaptation: HIIT trains high-intensity capacity, LISS builds easy aerobic volume, and Zone 2 targets sustainable aerobic base. The right mix depends on goals, recovery, and preference.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Cold Exposure — Benefits and Protocols
nodes.label: Cold Exposure — Benefits and Protocols
nodes.excerpt: Cold exposure may help alertness, perceived recovery, and cold tolerance, but evidence for broad health claims is still limited and risks are real. Protocols should be conservative, brief, and avoided by people with relevant cardiovascular or medical risk unless cleared.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Compound Lifts (Squat, Deadlift, Bench Press, OHP)
nodes.label: Compound Lifts (Squat, Deadlift, Bench Press, OHP)
nodes.excerpt: Compound lifts train multiple joints and muscle groups at once, making them efficient strength and muscle-building movements. Squat, deadlift, bench press, and overhead press are common anchors but not mandatory for every person.

nodes.label: Deload Weeks
nodes.excerpt: A deload week intentionally reduces training stress to dissipate fatigue while preserving momentum. Deloads are useful when fatigue accumulates, performance stalls, or a program has run hard for several weeks.

nodes.label: Exercise Science Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Exercise science studies how the body adapts to training stress through strength, endurance, mobility, recovery, and energy systems. Useful programming balances overload, specificity, fatigue, and consistency.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Eye Strain and Screen Time (20-20-20 Rule)
nodes.label: Eye Strain and Screen Time (20-20-20 Rule)
nodes.excerpt: Eye strain from screen work often comes from sustained near focus, glare, dryness, poor lighting, and long uninterrupted sessions. The 20-20-20 rule is a simple break habit: every 20 minutes, look 20 feet away for about 20 seconds.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Fasting Protocols for Health vs Performance
nodes.label: Fasting Protocols for Health vs Performance
nodes.excerpt: Fasting protocols can support calorie control or metabolic goals for some people, but they may conflict with training performance, recovery, medication timing, or disordered eating risk. The protocol should match the goal and individual context.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Heart Rate Variability (HRV) as a Recovery Metric
nodes.label: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) as a Recovery Metric
nodes.excerpt: Heart rate variability reflects variation between heartbeats and is often used as a recovery and stress signal. It is useful as a trend, not a single-day verdict, and should be interpreted alongside sleep, mood, performance, and illness.

nodes.label: Hypertrophy vs Strength Programming
nodes.excerpt: Hypertrophy programming prioritizes muscle growth, while strength programming prioritizes force output in specific movements. They overlap, but differ in volume, intensity, exercise selection, and progression.

nodes.label: Mobility and Flexibility Training
nodes.excerpt: Mobility is active control through range of motion, while flexibility is the available passive range. Good training combines strength, control, and appropriate stretching rather than chasing range alone.

nodes.label: NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis)
nodes.excerpt: NEAT is the energy used for non-exercise movement like walking, standing, chores, fidgeting, and daily activity. It can meaningfully affect energy expenditure and health without feeling like formal workouts.

nodes.label: Overtraining vs Overreaching
nodes.excerpt: Overreaching is short-term accumulated fatigue that can be recovered from, while overtraining is a more serious maladaptive state from prolonged stress and insufficient recovery. The distinction matters for programming and health.

nodes.label: Posture and Desk Ergonomics
nodes.excerpt: Desk ergonomics reduces strain by matching monitor, chair, keyboard, mouse, lighting, and break habits to the body. The best posture is variable: comfort comes from movement and setup, not holding one perfect position.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Recovery — Sleep, Nutrition, Active Recovery
nodes.label: Recovery — Sleep, Nutrition, Active Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Recovery is the process that turns training stress into adaptation. Sleep, nutrition, hydration, low-intensity movement, stress management, and appropriate programming all determine whether work becomes progress.

nodes.label: Sauna — Evidence and Use
nodes.excerpt: Sauna bathing is associated with cardiovascular and relaxation benefits, but much evidence is observational and individual safety matters. It should be treated as a heat-stress recovery tool, not a cure-all.

nodes.label: Step Count and NEAT
nodes.excerpt: Step count is a practical proxy for everyday movement and NEAT. It supports health, energy expenditure, and desk-work recovery without requiring every activity to become structured exercise.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Strength Training Principles (Progressive Overload, Specificity)
nodes.label: Strength Training Principles (Progressive Overload, Specificity)
nodes.excerpt: Strength training improves force production by progressively challenging muscles and nervous system patterns. The core principles are overload, specificity, sufficient volume, technical consistency, and recovery.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Stretching — Static vs Dynamic vs PNF
nodes.label: Stretching — Static vs Dynamic vs PNF
nodes.excerpt: Static, dynamic, and PNF stretching use different methods to improve range of motion or prepare for activity. Dynamic stretching fits warmups, static stretching fits flexibility work, and PNF uses contraction-relaxation patterns.

nodes.label: Sunlight Exposure and Health
nodes.excerpt: Sunlight supports circadian rhythm, mood, and vitamin D synthesis, but ultraviolet exposure also increases skin cancer and eye-disease risk. The practical balance is morning light, outdoor time, and sun protection when UV exposure is significant.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/VO2 Max — What It Is and How to Improve It
nodes.label: VO2 Max — What It Is and How to Improve It
nodes.excerpt: VO2 max measures the maximum rate of oxygen use during intense exercise and is a strong marker of aerobic fitness. It improves through consistent aerobic training, intervals, and enough recovery.

nodes.label: Wrist Health for Programmers
nodes.excerpt: Wrist health for programmers depends on input ergonomics, varied movement, reasonable workload, and early response to discomfort. The goal is reducing repetitive strain before it becomes persistent pain.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Zone 2 Cardio — Science and Benefits
nodes.label: Zone 2 Cardio — Science and Benefits
nodes.excerpt: Zone 2 cardio is sustainable aerobic work that improves mitochondrial function, fat oxidation, endurance base, and recovery capacity. It should feel controlled enough to maintain for a long duration.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Bell Labs and Its Innovations
nodes.label: Bell Labs and Its Innovations
nodes.excerpt: Bell Labs was one of the most productive industrial research labs in history, contributing the transistor, information theory, Unix, C, lasers, cellular concepts, and major physics discoveries. It shows what long-horizon research institutions can produce.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Artificial Intelligence (1950s-Present)
nodes.label: History of Artificial Intelligence (1950s-Present)
nodes.excerpt: AI history moves through symbolic AI, expert systems, AI winters, statistical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and foundation models. The field repeatedly cycles between ambition, disappointment, new methods, and scale.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Cloud Computing
nodes.label: History of Cloud Computing
nodes.excerpt: Cloud computing evolved from time-sharing, virtualization, hosting, and utility computing into on-demand infrastructure, platforms, serverless, managed databases, and global developer services. AWS popularized the modern cloud model.

nodes.label: History of Cryptography
nodes.excerpt: Cryptography evolved from hand ciphers and wartime codebreaking into public-key systems, internet security, digital signatures, cryptocurrencies, and privacy-preserving protocols. Its history tracks the tension between secrecy, trust, commerce, and state power.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Mobile Computing
nodes.label: History of Mobile Computing
nodes.excerpt: Mobile computing developed through laptops, PDAs, cellular networks, BlackBerry-style messaging, smartphones, app stores, and sensor-rich always-connected devices. It changed software around location, cameras, notifications, and constant presence.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Open Source
nodes.label: History of Open Source
nodes.excerpt: Open source grew from academic and hacker sharing cultures into a dominant software production model. It includes free software ideals, pragmatic open-source licensing, Linux, Apache, Git, GitHub, and modern dependency ecosystems.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Personal Computing (Altair to iPhone)
nodes.label: History of Personal Computing (Altair to iPhone)
nodes.excerpt: Personal computing moved computers from institutions to individuals through hobbyist kits, Apple, IBM PCs, GUIs, laptops, the internet, and smartphones. The iPhone completed a shift toward always-connected pocket computing.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Programming Languages
nodes.label: History of Programming Languages
nodes.excerpt: Programming languages evolved from machine code and assembly into high-level, structured, object-oriented, functional, scripting, systems, and domain-specific languages. Each generation traded control, safety, expressiveness, performance, and developer speed differently.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Search Engines
nodes.label: History of Search Engines
nodes.excerpt: Search engines evolved from human directories and early crawlers into ranking systems, ad platforms, vertical search, semantic search, and AI-assisted retrieval. The key shift was from indexing pages to ranking relevance at web scale.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Social Media
nodes.label: History of Social Media
nodes.excerpt: Social media evolved from forums, blogs, and early networks into algorithmic feeds, creator economies, messaging platforms, and attention markets. Its history is also a history of identity, virality, moderation, and surveillance advertising.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Video Game Consoles
nodes.label: History of Video Game Consoles
nodes.excerpt: Video game console history moves through arcade-inspired home systems, the 1983 crash, Nintendo's revival, 3D graphics, optical media, online services, motion controls, and hybrid hardware. Consoles are shaped by hardware cycles, exclusive games, distribution, and living-room culture.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Video Games
nodes.label: History of Video Games
nodes.excerpt: Video game history runs from arcade experiments and home consoles through PC gaming, 3D graphics, online play, mobile games, esports, streaming, and live-service economies. It is both technology history and culture history.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Internet (ARPANET to Web3)
nodes.label: History of the Internet (ARPANET to Web3)
nodes.excerpt: The internet evolved from packet-switching research and ARPANET into a global network built on TCP/IP, DNS, email, the web, broadband, mobile, cloud, and newer decentralization claims. Its history is a story of open protocols, institutions, commercialization, and power shifts.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Transistor and Integrated Circuit
nodes.label: History of the Transistor and Integrated Circuit
nodes.excerpt: The transistor replaced vacuum tubes and made modern electronics possible, while the integrated circuit packed many components onto one chip. Together they enabled computing, consumer electronics, telecommunications, and Moore's Law scaling.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Web (Web 1.0 to 2.0 to 3.0)
nodes.label: History of the Web (Web 1.0 to 2.0 to 3.0)
nodes.excerpt: The web evolved from linked documents into interactive platforms, social applications, cloud-backed SaaS, and contested Web3 decentralization narratives. Its history is distinct from the internet: the web is one application layer built on internet protocols.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Moore's Law — History and Decline
nodes.label: Moore's Law — History and Decline
nodes.excerpt: Moore's Law described the repeated doubling of transistor density, driving decades of cheaper and faster computing. Its slowing has shifted progress toward specialization, parallelism, chiplets, accelerators, and software efficiency.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Rise of the Smartphone (The iPhone Moment)
nodes.label: Rise of the Smartphone (The iPhone Moment)
nodes.excerpt: The smartphone rose from earlier mobile phones, PDAs, BlackBerry, and touch devices, but the iPhone crystallized a new model: multitouch, full web, sensors, app ecosystem, and pocket computing as daily infrastructure.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
nodes.label: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
nodes.excerpt: The age of surveillance capitalism describes business models that extract, predict, and shape behavior through data collection and targeted advertising. The concept is associated with Shoshana Zuboff and helps explain platform incentives.

nodes.label: The Browser Wars
nodes.excerpt: The browser wars were battles over the web's main user interface, from Netscape vs Internet Explorer to later competition involving Firefox, Chrome, Safari, and Edge. They shaped standards, performance, security, and platform power.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Dotcom Boom and Bust
nodes.label: The Dotcom Boom and Bust
nodes.excerpt: The dotcom boom and bust was a late-1990s internet investment bubble followed by a crash, but the underlying infrastructure and user behavior kept growing. It is a classic case of real technology plus unsustainable speculation.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Homebrew Computer Club
nodes.label: The Homebrew Computer Club
nodes.excerpt: The Homebrew Computer Club was a Silicon Valley hobbyist community that helped launch personal computing culture. It connected hardware tinkerers, software enthusiasts, and early entrepreneurs, including Apple founders.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Open Source Movement (Linux, GNU, Apache)
nodes.label: The Open Source Movement (Linux, GNU, Apache)
nodes.excerpt: The open source movement blends free software ideals with practical collaborative development. GNU, Linux, Apache, BSD, and later GitHub made open infrastructure central to modern computing.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Xerox PARC — Ideas That Changed Computing
nodes.label: Xerox PARC — Ideas That Changed Computing
nodes.excerpt: Xerox PARC pioneered or refined key personal-computing ideas: graphical interfaces, Ethernet, laser printing, object-oriented environments, WYSIWYG editing, and networked workstations. Its story is also a lesson in invention versus commercialization.

nodes.label: IaaS PaaS SaaS
nodes.excerpt: service model spectrum: IaaS (raw VMs), PaaS (managed runtime), SaaS (fully managed app), FaaS (functions), and where the lines blur.

nodes.label: Interview Communication
nodes.excerpt: interview communication guide covering project explanations, STAR stories, coding/system design structure, debugging out loud, clarifying questions, recovery, and resume bullet framing.

nodes.label: Kubernetes
nodes.excerpt: desired state reconciliation, control plane, worker nodes, all core objects (Pod/Deployment/Service/Ingress/StatefulSet/HPA/PV), scheduling, health probes, networking (CNI/kube-proxy/CoreDNS), RBAC.

nodes.label: LLM Capabilities and AI Ethics
nodes.excerpt: opinionated overview of what LLMs can and cannot do, their tradeoffs, whether they replace engineers/humans, and the philosophy, morality, and ethics of AI deployment.

nodes.label: LLM System Design
nodes.excerpt: tokens, context windows, prompt caching (KV cache, TTL, cost math), streaming, tool use/agentic loops, RAG (chunking, hybrid search, reranking), multi-agent orchestration, evals, fine-tuning vs prompting.

nodes.label: Effective Vocabulary Building
nodes.excerpt: Effective vocabulary building means learning words as usable distinctions, not isolated definitions. Durable vocabulary comes from context, examples, retrieval, morphology, and active use in speech or writing.

nodes.label: Etymology — How Words Evolve
nodes.excerpt: Etymology studies how words change through borrowing, sound shifts, metaphor, semantic drift, and cultural contact. It is useful because word histories reveal hidden connections, but current meaning still depends on present usage.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/How Language Works (Syntax, Semantics, Pragmatics)
nodes.label: How Language Works (Syntax, Semantics, Pragmatics)
nodes.excerpt: Language works through layered systems: syntax structures sentences, semantics carries meaning, and pragmatics explains how context changes what utterances actually do. This note gives Bobby a compact map for reasoning about communication, ambiguity, and human meaning-making.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/Language Acquisition — How Humans Learn Language
nodes.label: Language Acquisition — How Humans Learn Language
nodes.excerpt: Language acquisition is the human process of learning sound patterns, words, grammar, and social use from rich interaction. It combines biological readiness, statistical learning, feedback, imitation, and meaningful communication.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/Language Learning Methods (Immersion, Comprehensible Input, Krashen)
nodes.label: Language Learning Methods (Immersion, Comprehensible Input, Krashen)
nodes.excerpt: Strong language learning combines comprehensible input, meaningful output, spaced review, pronunciation practice, and real interaction. Krashen's input hypothesis is useful because learners need language they mostly understand, but fluency also benefits from deliberate output and feedback.

nodes.label: Natural Language Processing Overview
nodes.excerpt: Natural language processing is the field of making computers process, search, generate, classify, translate, and reason over human language. Modern NLP is dominated by embeddings and transformer language models, but classic tasks still depend on data quality, evaluation, and linguistic ambiguity.

nodes.label: Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis (Language Shapes Thought)
nodes.excerpt: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis asks whether language shapes thought, with a strong deterministic version mostly rejected and a weaker influence version still useful. The practical takeaway is that words and categories can guide attention without fully trapping cognition.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/Writing Systems of the World
nodes.label: Writing Systems of the World
nodes.excerpt: Writing systems encode language in different ways: alphabets represent sounds, syllabaries represent syllables, abjads emphasize consonants, and logographic systems represent morphemes or words. Understanding writing systems helps explain literacy, typography, input methods, and why text is not language itself.

nodes.label: 1-on-1 Meeting Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: 1-on-1 meetings are recurring manager-report conversations for support, context, feedback, career development, and trust. The best ones are employee-centered, consistent, and action-oriented.

nodes.label: Async Communication Culture
nodes.excerpt: Async communication culture uses written context, clear ownership, durable decisions, and respectful response expectations so teams can coordinate without constant meetings. It works best with strong writing and explicit norms.

nodes.label: Conway's Law
nodes.excerpt: Conway's Law says organizations tend to design systems that mirror their communication structures. Architecture and org design influence each other because teams build along coordination boundaries.

nodes.label: Decision-Making Frameworks (DACI, RACI)
nodes.excerpt: DACI and RACI clarify roles in decisions and execution. They reduce ambiguity by naming who drives, approves, contributes, is informed, is responsible, accountable, consulted, or informed.

nodes.label: Delegation — When and How
nodes.excerpt: Delegation transfers ownership of outcomes, decisions, or tasks to create leverage and growth. Good delegation clarifies context, authority, expectations, support, and feedback loops.

nodes.label: Engineering Levels and Ladders
nodes.excerpt: Engineering levels and ladders define expectations for impact, scope, autonomy, technical judgment, collaboration, and leadership across career stages. They make growth and promotion criteria more explicit.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Engineering Management vs IC Track
nodes.label: Engineering Management vs IC Track
nodes.excerpt: Engineering management and individual contributor tracks are distinct leadership paths. Managers lead through people, systems, and priorities; senior ICs lead through technical judgment, architecture, execution, and influence.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Giving Feedback (SBI Model — Situation, Behavior, Impact)
nodes.label: Giving Feedback (SBI Model — Situation, Behavior, Impact)
nodes.excerpt: The SBI feedback model structures feedback around the situation, observed behavior, and impact. It reduces vague judgment by grounding feedback in specific events and consequences.

nodes.label: Hiring — Structured Interviews
nodes.excerpt: Structured interviews use consistent criteria, questions, rubrics, and evidence collection to evaluate candidates more fairly and predictively. They reduce bias compared with improvised interview conversations.

nodes.label: Managing Up
nodes.excerpt: Managing up means proactively helping a manager make better decisions by clarifying goals, surfacing risks, communicating progress, understanding preferences, and asking for needed support. It is not manipulation; it is upward coordination.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Organizational Design (Functional vs Divisional vs Matrix)
nodes.label: Organizational Design (Functional vs Divisional vs Matrix)
nodes.excerpt: Organizational design chooses how work, authority, communication, and accountability are structured. Functional, divisional, and matrix models trade specialization, speed, coordination, and clarity.

nodes.label: Performance Reviews
nodes.excerpt: Performance reviews summarize impact, behavior, growth, expectations, and compensation or promotion implications over a defined period. Good reviews are evidence-based and should not surprise the person being reviewed.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Psychological Safety in Teams (Amy Edmondson)
nodes.label: Psychological Safety in Teams (Amy Edmondson)
nodes.excerpt: Psychological safety is a shared belief that people can speak up, ask questions, admit mistakes, and challenge ideas without interpersonal punishment. It supports learning, quality, and risk detection.

nodes.label: Radical Candor (Kim Scott)
nodes.excerpt: Radical Candor combines caring personally with challenging directly. The framework warns against ruinous empathy, obnoxious aggression, manipulative insincerity, and silence disguised as kindness.

nodes.label: Remote Team Management
nodes.excerpt: Remote team management requires deliberate communication, documentation, trust, rituals, timezone awareness, outcome focus, and social connection. It exposes weak management habits that offices can sometimes hide.

nodes.label: Running Effective Meetings
nodes.excerpt: Effective meetings have a clear purpose, right participants, useful preparation, active facilitation, explicit decisions, and follow-up. Meetings are expensive coordination tools, not default containers for work.

nodes.label: Technical Interview Design
nodes.excerpt: Technical interview design defines how to evaluate engineering candidates against job-relevant skills with structured tasks, rubrics, calibration, and candidate experience. Good interviews test work signal rather than puzzle performance alone.

nodes.label: The Principal-Agent Problem
nodes.excerpt: The principal-agent problem occurs when someone acts on behalf of another but has different incentives, information, or risk exposure. It appears in management, sales, finance, politics, and contracting.

nodes.label: The Staff Engineer's Path
nodes.excerpt: The Staff Engineer path is a senior individual-contributor track focused on technical leadership, cross-team influence, architecture, execution, mentorship, and organizational leverage without direct management authority.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Active Recall vs Passive Review
nodes.label: Active Recall vs Passive Review
nodes.excerpt: Active recall strengthens memory by forcing retrieval, while passive review mainly creates familiarity. Durable learning comes from trying to remember before looking at the answer.

nodes.label: Anki — Optimal Card Design
nodes.excerpt: Good Anki cards test one clear idea, use active recall, and minimize ambiguity. Card quality matters more than deck size because vague cards train frustration instead of memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Calibration — Knowing What You Don't Know
nodes.label: Calibration — Knowing What You Don't Know
nodes.excerpt: Calibration is the skill of matching confidence to reality. It improves through prediction, feedback, decision journaling, and noticing where understanding feels fluent but fails under retrieval or application.

nodes.label: Chunking Information
nodes.excerpt: Chunking groups smaller pieces of information into meaningful units so working memory can handle them more efficiently. Expertise often looks like larger and better-organized chunks.

nodes.label: Deliberate Practice (Anders Ericsson)
nodes.excerpt: Deliberate practice is focused practice designed to improve a specific weakness with feedback, challenge, and repetition. It differs from simply spending time because it targets the edge of current ability.

nodes.label: Desirable Difficulties in Learning
nodes.excerpt: Desirable difficulties are learning conditions that feel harder in the short term but improve long-term retention and transfer. Examples include spacing, retrieval, interleaving, variation, and generation.

nodes.label: First Principles Thinking
nodes.excerpt: First principles thinking breaks a problem down to basic truths and rebuilds from there. It is useful when analogies, conventions, or inherited assumptions are constraining the solution.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Growth Mindset vs Fixed Mindset
nodes.label: Growth Mindset vs Fixed Mindset
nodes.excerpt: Growth mindset frames ability as improvable through effort, strategy, and feedback, while fixed mindset treats ability as static. The useful version is not empty positivity; it is choosing better practice after failure.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How Memory Works (Encoding, Storage, Retrieval)
nodes.label: How Memory Works (Encoding, Storage, Retrieval)
nodes.excerpt: Memory involves encoding information, storing it over time, and retrieving it later. Learning improves when attention, meaning, retrieval practice, spacing, and context are designed deliberately.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How to Read a Technical Paper
nodes.label: How to Read a Technical Paper
nodes.excerpt: Reading a technical paper is an active process: identify the question, contribution, method, assumptions, evidence, limitations, and relevance before getting lost in details. Multiple passes usually beat one slow linear read.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How to Retain What You Read
nodes.label: How to Retain What You Read
nodes.excerpt: Retaining what you read requires active processing: previewing, questioning, summarizing, recalling, connecting, and applying. Highlighting alone creates familiarity but weak long-term recall.

nodes.label: Interleaving Practice
nodes.excerpt: Interleaving practice mixes related problem types instead of drilling one type in a block. It improves discrimination because learners must decide which strategy applies, not just repeat the obvious one.

nodes.label: Inversion Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Inversion thinking solves problems by asking what would cause failure, then avoiding or counteracting those causes. It is useful because failure modes are often easier to identify than perfect solutions.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Learning a New Domain Fast
nodes.label: Learning a New Domain Fast
nodes.excerpt: Learning a new domain fast means building a map of core concepts, vocabulary, constraints, experts, tools, and feedback loops. The fastest path combines overview, targeted projects, active recall, and conversations with knowledgeable people.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Learning a New Programming Language Fast
nodes.label: Learning a New Programming Language Fast
nodes.excerpt: Learning a programming language fast means focusing on syntax, type system, runtime model, package workflow, idioms, and small real programs. The fastest route is comparing it to languages already known while building something concrete.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Mental Models — What They Are and How to Build Them
nodes.label: Mental Models — What They Are and How to Build Them
nodes.excerpt: Mental models are reusable representations for understanding how something works or what matters in a situation. They improve judgment when they are accurate, connected, and tested against reality.

nodes.label: Metacognition
nodes.excerpt: Metacognition is thinking about one's own thinking: planning, monitoring, and evaluating learning or decision processes. It helps learners choose strategies intentionally instead of confusing effort with progress.

nodes.label: Note-Taking Methods (Cornell, Outline, Zettelkasten)
nodes.excerpt: Note-taking methods differ in how they structure attention, recall, and synthesis. Cornell supports study review, outlines support hierarchy, and Zettelkasten supports connected long-term thinking.

nodes.label: Productive Disagreement
nodes.excerpt: Productive disagreement turns conflict into better understanding by separating claims, evidence, assumptions, and values. It works when participants try to improve the model rather than win the exchange.

nodes.label: Second-Order Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Second-order thinking looks beyond the immediate outcome to the downstream consequences of a decision. It is useful for avoiding fixes that solve today's problem by creating tomorrow's.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Spaced Repetition — Theory and Implementation
nodes.label: Spaced Repetition — Theory and Implementation
nodes.excerpt: Spaced repetition schedules reviews just before forgetting so memories are strengthened efficiently. It works best for durable facts, concepts, prompts, and procedures that benefit from repeated retrieval.

nodes.label: Systems Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Systems thinking studies relationships, feedback loops, delays, incentives, and boundaries rather than isolated parts. It is useful for understanding why local fixes can fail in complex environments.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Teaching as a Learning Tool
nodes.label: Teaching as a Learning Tool
nodes.excerpt: Teaching strengthens learning because it forces retrieval, organization, simplification, and feedback. Explaining a topic clearly reveals missing links that passive review can hide.

nodes.label: The Feynman Technique
nodes.excerpt: The Feynman Technique tests understanding by explaining a concept simply, finding gaps, revisiting the source, and refining the explanation. It is useful because clear explanation exposes fuzzy knowledge.

nodes.label: The Learning Pyramid
nodes.excerpt: The Learning Pyramid is a popular but oversimplified model claiming different retention rates by activity. Its useful takeaway is that active methods often beat passive review, but the exact percentages should not be treated as evidence.

nodes.label: Transfer of Learning
nodes.excerpt: Transfer of learning is applying knowledge or skill from one context to another. It improves when learners understand underlying principles, practice varied examples, and compare cases instead of memorizing surface patterns.

nodes.label: MLOps and ML Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ML lifecycle from data and labels through training, experiment tracking, model registry, deployment, monitoring, drift, retraining, and LLMOps for Bobby-like systems.

nodes.label: Bayes' Theorem
nodes.excerpt: Bayes' theorem updates belief in a hypothesis after observing evidence. It relates prior probability, likelihood, evidence probability, and posterior probability.

nodes.label: Big-O Notation and Complexity Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Big-O notation describes how algorithm cost grows as input size increases. Complexity analysis helps compare approaches by time, space, and scalability rather than by machine-specific timing.

nodes.label: Boolean Algebra and Logic Gates
nodes.excerpt: Boolean algebra reasons about true/false values with operations such as AND, OR, NOT, XOR, NAND, and NOR. Logic gates implement these operations in hardware and shape digital circuits, conditionals, filters, and bit manipulation.

nodes.label: Calculus — Derivatives and Integrals
nodes.excerpt: Calculus studies change and accumulation through derivatives and integrals. Derivatives measure local rate of change, while integrals measure accumulated quantity over an interval.

nodes.label: Central Limit Theorem
nodes.excerpt: The Central Limit Theorem says that sums or averages of many independent random variables tend toward a normal distribution under broad conditions. It explains why normal approximations appear so often in statistics.

nodes.label: Chain Rule (in Context of Backprop)
nodes.excerpt: The chain rule computes derivatives of composed functions by multiplying local derivative effects. Backpropagation applies the chain rule through a neural network so each parameter receives its contribution to the loss.

nodes.label: Combinatorics Basics (Permutations, Combinations)
nodes.excerpt: Combinatorics counts arrangements and selections. Permutations care about order, combinations do not, and both are foundational for probability, algorithm analysis, search spaces, and counting arguments.

nodes.label: Discrete Mathematics for CS
nodes.excerpt: Discrete mathematics studies countable structures such as sets, logic, graphs, integers, relations, functions, and combinatorics. It is the mathematical foundation for algorithms, data structures, databases, cryptography, and formal reasoning.

nodes.label: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
nodes.excerpt: Eigenvectors are directions that a linear transformation only scales, and eigenvalues are the scaling factors. They reveal hidden structure in matrices and appear in PCA, graph analysis, differential equations, and stability reasoning.

nodes.label: Expected Value and Variance
nodes.excerpt: Expected value is the long-run average outcome of a random variable, while variance measures how spread out outcomes are around that average. Together they summarize central tendency and uncertainty.

nodes.label: Fourier Transform Intuition
nodes.excerpt: The Fourier transform represents signals as combinations of frequencies instead of values over time or space. It is useful for audio, images, compression, filtering, signal processing, and understanding periodic structure.

nodes.label: Gradient and Partial Derivatives
nodes.excerpt: Partial derivatives measure how a multivariable function changes with respect to one input, and the gradient collects those derivatives into a vector. Gradients drive optimization by pointing toward steepest local increase.

nodes.label: Graph Theory Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Graph theory studies nodes and edges, making it the natural language for networks, dependencies, maps, social systems, state spaces, and many algorithms. Core ideas include directedness, weights, paths, cycles, connectivity, trees, and traversal.

nodes.id: Resources/Mathematics/Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence
nodes.label: Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence
nodes.excerpt: Information theory measures uncertainty, surprise, and difference between probability distributions. Entropy, cross-entropy, and KL divergence are central to compression, machine learning losses, language models, and variational inference.

nodes.label: Lagrange Multipliers
nodes.excerpt: Lagrange multipliers solve constrained optimization problems by combining an objective with constraint equations. At an optimum, the objective gradient aligns with constraint gradients under regularity conditions.

nodes.label: Law of Large Numbers
nodes.excerpt: The Law of Large Numbers says sample averages converge toward expected value as sample size grows. It explains why repeated measurement and large samples reduce random noise, while not eliminating bias.

nodes.label: Linear Algebra — Vectors and Matrices
nodes.excerpt: Linear algebra studies vectors, matrices, linear transformations, and systems of equations. It is the mathematical language behind graphics, machine learning, optimization, embeddings, and many engineering models.

nodes.label: Markov Chains
nodes.excerpt: Markov chains model systems that move between states where the next state depends only on the current state. They are useful for queues, random walks, language modeling history, simulations, ranking, and stochastic processes.

nodes.label: Matrix Multiplication and Dot Products
nodes.excerpt: Dot products measure alignment between vectors, while matrix multiplication composes linear transformations and combines rows with columns. These operations power projections, neural network layers, graphics transforms, and similarity search.

nodes.label: Monte Carlo Methods
nodes.excerpt: Monte Carlo methods use random sampling to estimate quantities that may be hard to compute exactly. They are useful for simulation, integration, uncertainty estimation, randomized algorithms, and probabilistic inference.

nodes.label: Multivariable Calculus Basics
nodes.excerpt: Multivariable calculus extends calculus to functions with multiple inputs or outputs. It introduces partial derivatives, gradients, Jacobians, Hessians, and constrained optimization.

nodes.label: Number Theory Basics (Primes, Modular Arithmetic)
nodes.excerpt: Number theory studies integers, divisibility, primes, congruences, and modular arithmetic. It underlies hashing, cryptography, randomized algorithms, checksums, and many programming puzzle patterns.

nodes.label: Optimization — Convex vs Non-Convex
nodes.excerpt: Optimization searches for inputs or parameters that minimize or maximize an objective. Convex optimization has nicer global guarantees, while non-convex optimization can contain local minima, saddle points, and difficult landscapes.

nodes.label: Probability Distributions (Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Exponential)
nodes.excerpt: Probability distributions describe how likely different outcomes are. Normal, binomial, Poisson, and exponential distributions model common patterns such as measurement noise, success counts, event counts, and waiting times.

nodes.label: Set Theory
nodes.excerpt: Set theory studies collections of distinct objects and operations such as union, intersection, difference, complement, and membership. It is a basic language for logic, databases, probability, type systems, and discrete math.

nodes.label: Mechanistic Interpretability
nodes.excerpt: reverse-engineering transformers: circuit hypothesis, induction heads, IOI circuit, superposition, sparse autoencoders, activation patching.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/ADHD — Understanding and Managing It
nodes.label: ADHD — Understanding and Managing It
nodes.excerpt: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition driven by dopamine/norepinephrine dysregulation in the prefrontal cortex and striatum — primarily an executive function disorder, not an attention deficit. Russell Barkley's model reframes it as impaired self-regulation and time blindness. Understanding the actual mechanism changes what management strategies are worth trying.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Anxiety — Types and Coping Mechanisms
nodes.label: Anxiety — Types and Coping Mechanisms
nodes.excerpt: Anxiety is the brain's threat-detection system running past its useful range — anticipating danger that either doesn't exist or isn't proportionate. Different anxiety disorders have distinct triggers and mechanisms, but all are maintained by the same core loop: avoidance prevents extinction and keeps fear alive. The most effective coping tools target this avoidance directly.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Attachment Styles (Secure, Anxious, Avoidant, Fearful)
nodes.label: Attachment Styles (Secure, Anxious, Avoidant, Fearful)
nodes.excerpt: Attachment theory (Bowlby, Ainsworth) describes how early caregiver relationships shape internal working models of closeness, safety, and self-worth in relationships. Four adult attachment styles — secure, anxious, avoidant, fearful — predict how people behave under relational stress, seek or reject support, and regulate emotions in close relationships. Styles are patterns, not fixed identities, and can shift with experience and therapy.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Burnout — Signs, Stages, and Recovery
nodes.label: Burnout — Signs, Stages, and Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Burnout is a syndrome of emotional exhaustion, cynicism/depersonalization, and reduced personal accomplishment resulting from chronic unmanaged workplace stress. Christina Maslach's 40+ years of research identifies six organizational misfits that cause it. Recovery requires structural change, not just rest — and often takes 6–12 months for full recovery.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Chronic Stress — Long-Term Physical and Mental Effects
nodes.label: Chronic Stress — Long-Term Physical and Mental Effects
nodes.excerpt: Chronic stress is the physiological and psychological state when the stress response activates more often than the body can recover from it. The long-term effects are systemic: cognitive degradation, cardiovascular damage, immune dysfunction, metabolic changes, and structural brain changes. Understanding the mechanism helps separate 'feeling stressed' from the actual biological load accumulating.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) Basics
nodes.label: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) Basics
nodes.excerpt: CBT is the most evidence-supported psychotherapy for anxiety, depression, and several other conditions. It works by interrupting the feedback loops between thoughts, emotions, body sensations, and behaviors — and teaching skills to respond differently. The core insight is that changing how you interpret an event changes how you feel about it.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Cognitive Distortions — Complete Reference
nodes.label: Cognitive Distortions — Complete Reference
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive distortions are habitual, automatic thought patterns that skew interpretation toward threat, failure, blame, or hopelessness. They originate from Aaron Beck's cognitive model and are the primary intervention target in CBT. Naming the pattern creates enough distance from the thought to examine it rather than automatically act on it.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Depression — Biology and Treatment
nodes.label: Depression — Biology and Treatment
nodes.excerpt: Major depressive disorder involves persistent low mood or anhedonia plus at least 4 additional symptoms for 2+ weeks, causing significant impairment. The biology is more complex than the 'chemical imbalance' framing: neuroinflammation, HPA dysregulation, and default mode network overactivity are all implicated. Treatment evidence supports CBT, behavioral activation, SSRIs/SNRIs, and exercise — with newer options (ketamine, TMS) for treatment-resistant cases.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Dopamine System — Rewards and Motivation
nodes.label: Dopamine System — Rewards and Motivation
nodes.excerpt: Dopamine is a neuromodulator involved in reward prediction error, motivation, and learning — not pleasure itself. Wolfram Schultz's Nobel-adjacent work showed dopamine neurons fire on prediction errors, not rewards per se. The wanting/liking distinction (Kent Berridge) is critical: dopamine drives wanting and pursuit, while opioid systems drive liking and enjoyment. This explains compulsive behavior without proportionate satisfaction.

nodes.label: Emotional Regulation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Emotional regulation is the ability to influence which emotions arise, when, how intensely, and how long they last — and then to respond from choice rather than automaticity. James Gross's process model identifies five intervention points. DBT's skills hierarchy (TIPP, STOP, PLEASE) gives a practical ladder. The key distinction: regulation is not suppression — it changes the emotion, not just the expression.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/FOMO and Digital Anxiety
nodes.label: FOMO and Digital Anxiety
nodes.excerpt: FOMO and digital anxiety arise when feeds, notifications, social comparison, and constant availability make missing information feel costly. The fix is not knowing everything, but setting boundaries around inputs and values.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Grief and Loss — Stages and Modern Research
nodes.label: Grief and Loss — Stages and Modern Research
nodes.excerpt: Grief is a varied response to loss, not a fixed five-stage checklist. Modern grief research emphasizes oscillation, meaning, continuing bonds, individual differences, and support rather than one universal timeline.

nodes.label: Impostor Syndrome
nodes.excerpt: Impostor syndrome (Clance & Imes, 1978) is the persistent internal experience of intellectual fraudulence despite objective evidence of competence. It is driven by a self-perpetuating cognitive cycle that attributes success to external factors and treats mistakes as evidence of true inadequacy. Highly prevalent in software engineering — especially among high performers, in new roles, or in underrepresented groups.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Journaling for Mental Health
nodes.label: Journaling for Mental Health
nodes.excerpt: Journaling has solid research support (Pennebaker's expressive writing paradigm) for processing difficult experiences and reducing their ongoing physiological impact. Different journaling formats serve different purposes — expressive writing, gratitude, CBT thought records, decision journaling. The key distinction: reflection that moves toward insight is different from venting that reinforces rumination.

nodes.label: Learned Helplessness
nodes.excerpt: Learned helplessness (Seligman, 1967) is the reduced motivation, cognitive impairment, and passive acceptance that results from repeated exposure to uncontrollable outcomes. The key insight: the animal/person doesn't learn 'I can't escape this' — they learn 'I can't control anything,' which generalizes beyond the original situation. The reformulated model (Abramson 1978) adds attribution style as the individual difference that determines who develops helplessness vs. who doesn't.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Locus of Control (Internal vs External)
nodes.label: Locus of Control (Internal vs External)
nodes.excerpt: Locus of control (Julian Rotter, 1966) describes the generalized expectancy about whether outcomes are determined by one's own behavior (internal) vs external forces (luck, others, fate). Internal LOC correlates with better health outcomes, academic achievement, and resilience. The nuance: healthy internality is specific and calibrated — not self-blame for genuinely uncontrollable events, but persistent agency over what can actually be influenced.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Loneliness and Connection — The Health Impact
nodes.label: Loneliness and Connection — The Health Impact
nodes.excerpt: Loneliness is the felt gap between desired and actual connection, and it is associated with worse mental and physical health. Connection is a health behavior, not just a nice extra.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Meditation Types and Benefits
nodes.label: Meditation Types and Benefits
nodes.excerpt: Meditation is a family of distinct practices that train different cognitive and emotional capacities. Focused attention trains sustained concentration. Open monitoring trains metacognitive awareness. Loving-kindness trains compassion and social warmth. Body scan trains interoception. Choosing the right type depends on what the person actually needs, not a generic 'try meditation' recommendation.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Mindfulness — Evidence and Practice
nodes.label: Mindfulness — Evidence and Practice
nodes.excerpt: Mindfulness is the practice of deliberately attending to present-moment experience — thoughts, sensations, emotions — with less automatic reactivity. Jon Kabat-Zinn operationalized it in 1979 as MBSR and launched three decades of neuroscience research showing measurable structural and functional brain changes. It is evidence-supported for stress, anxiety, depression relapse, and pain — but not a universal treatment and requires care around trauma.

nodes.label: Neuroplasticity
nodes.excerpt: Neuroplasticity is the nervous system's capacity to change its structure and function through experience. It includes synaptic strengthening/weakening, structural growth, and adult neurogenesis in the hippocampus. The key principles: change is specific (practice what you want to change), change requires attention and salience, sleep consolidates it, and BDNF is the primary molecular signal. Chronic stress impairs plasticity; exercise, learning, and sleep promote it.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Perfectionism — Causes and Fixes
nodes.label: Perfectionism — Causes and Fixes
nodes.excerpt: Perfectionism is not high standards — it is a pattern where self-worth becomes contingent on achievement quality, mistakes feel threatening rather than informative, and avoidance becomes preferable to imperfect output. Frost's Multidimensional Perfectionism Scale identifies six components. Maladaptive perfectionism is a primary driver of procrastination, burnout, and chronic underperformance despite high ability.

nodes.label: Post-Traumatic Growth
nodes.excerpt: Post-traumatic growth (PTG) is positive psychological change that some people report following a struggle with highly challenging life circumstances. Tedeschi and Calhoun (1996) identified five domains: personal strength, relating to others, new possibilities, appreciation of life, and spiritual/existential change. The mechanism is shattered-assumptions rebuilding — not that trauma is good, but that rebuilding core belief structures after their destruction sometimes creates more robust ones.

nodes.label: Psychological Safety
nodes.excerpt: Psychological safety (Amy Edmondson, Harvard) is the shared belief that a team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — speaking up, admitting mistakes, asking questions, and challenging ideas without fear of punishment or humiliation. Google's Project Aristotle identified it as the single strongest predictor of team performance. It is created primarily through leader behavior, especially how leaders respond to bad news.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Resilience — How to Build It
nodes.label: Resilience — How to Build It
nodes.excerpt: Resilience is the capacity to absorb adversity, adapt, and maintain or regain function — not the absence of distress. Research (Bonanno, APA, van der Kolk) shows resilience is not a fixed trait but a dynamic resource built through specific factors: relationships, recovery, meaning, flexible thinking, and graduated exposure to manageable challenges. It can be built deliberately.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Self-Compassion vs Self-Esteem (Kristin Neff)
nodes.label: Self-Compassion vs Self-Esteem (Kristin Neff)
nodes.excerpt: Self-compassion (Kristin Neff) means treating yourself during difficulty with the same kindness you'd offer a good friend — not excusing poor performance, but responding to failure and pain without harsh self-attack. Unlike self-esteem, it doesn't require positive self-evaluation to maintain. Research shows it predicts lower shame, less fear of failure, more motivation to improve, and better resilience than high self-esteem.

nodes.label: Serotonin System
nodes.excerpt: Serotonin (5-HT) is a neurotransmitter and signaling molecule synthesized from tryptophan, with 14+ receptor subtypes and roles spanning mood, sleep, appetite, pain, gut motility, social behavior, and cognition. ~90% of the body's serotonin is in the gut. The serotonin-depression link is more complex than 'low serotonin = depression' — a 2022 umbrella review found no consistent evidence for the serotonin deficiency theory of depression.

nodes.label: Social Comparison Theory
nodes.excerpt: Leon Festinger's 1954 social comparison theory holds that people evaluate their opinions and abilities by comparing themselves to others, especially under uncertainty. Upward comparison motivates but can produce envy/inadequacy; downward comparison boosts mood but can produce complacency. Social media amplifies harmful comparison by providing curated upward comparisons at scale. Alternative comparison targets (past self, future self) are often more productive.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Stoicism Applied to Modern Life
nodes.label: Stoicism Applied to Modern Life
nodes.excerpt: Applied Stoicism focuses on what is within one's control, responding to events with virtue and judgment, and practicing perspective before reaction. Its modern value is emotional discipline without emotional suppression.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Stress Response — HPA Axis (Cortisol Cascade)
nodes.label: Stress Response — HPA Axis (Cortisol Cascade)
nodes.excerpt: The stress response involves two cascading systems: the fast SAM axis (seconds, adrenaline) and the slower HPA axis (minutes to hours, cortisol). Both are adaptive short-term but damaging when chronically activated. Understanding the HPA axis explains why sleep, mood, immunity, memory, and metabolism all degrade under sustained stress.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/The Role of Play in Adult Life
nodes.label: The Role of Play in Adult Life
nodes.excerpt: Play is voluntary, intrinsically motivated activity where the process is the point — not performed for external reward. Stuart Brown's research identifies seven distinct play types. The neuroscience: play activates dopaminergic circuits, promotes prefrontal plasticity, and reduces cortisol. Adults who maintain play capacity show better creativity, resilience, and emotional regulation. The anti-optimization framing: not every activity needs to produce output.

nodes.label: Message Queues
nodes.excerpt: delivery guarantees (at-most/at-least/exactly-once), Kafka internals (partitions, offsets, consumer groups, zero-copy, EOS), SQS, RabbitMQ, outbox pattern, event sourcing, backpressure.

nodes.label: Calibration and Epistemic Humility
nodes.excerpt: Calibration means your confidence matches reality over time: 70 percent confidence should be right about 70 percent of the time. Epistemic humility is the discipline of treating beliefs as improvable models rather than identity claims.

nodes.label: Fermi Estimation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Fermi estimation is a way to approximate unknown quantities by decomposing them into smaller guesses and orders of magnitude. It is useful for sanity-checking claims, sizing systems, making decisions with incomplete data, and catching impossible numbers early.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Build a Mental Model
nodes.label: How to Build a Mental Model
nodes.excerpt: A mental model is a compressed representation of how something works, useful because it lets you predict, explain, and act without memorizing every detail. Building one means identifying the system boundaries, core variables, causal relationships, feedback loops, failure modes, and places where the model breaks.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Find the Crux of a Disagreement
nodes.label: How to Find the Crux of a Disagreement
nodes.excerpt: The crux of a disagreement is the belief that would actually change someone's conclusion if it changed. Finding it requires separating definitions, values, evidence, predictions, and trust judgments instead of arguing around surface claims.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Read Primary Research (Not Just Summaries)
nodes.label: How to Read Primary Research (Not Just Summaries)
nodes.excerpt: Reading primary research means inspecting the actual question, methods, data, results, limitations, and incentives rather than relying only on abstracts or popular summaries. The goal is to understand what the study really supports, what it does not, and how much confidence it deserves.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Update Beliefs Gracefully (Bayesian Updating)
nodes.label: How to Update Beliefs Gracefully (Bayesian Updating)
nodes.excerpt: Bayesian updating is the habit of changing confidence when new evidence arrives, rather than flipping between certainty and rejection. Updating gracefully means treating belief changes as maintenance on your model, not as a personal defeat.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Write an Evergreen Note
nodes.label: How to Write an Evergreen Note
nodes.excerpt: An evergreen note is a durable, self-contained idea written so it can be reused, linked, and improved over time. Good evergreen notes have a clear claim, context, reasoning, examples, and connections instead of being raw highlights or vague summaries.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/The Difference Between Information and Knowledge
nodes.label: The Difference Between Information and Knowledge
nodes.excerpt: Information is available content; knowledge is organized, interpreted, connected, and usable understanding. The difference matters because collecting more notes, tabs, or facts does not automatically improve judgment unless the material changes what you can explain, predict, or do.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/The Map is Not the Territory
nodes.label: The Map is Not the Territory
nodes.excerpt: `The map is not the territory` means models, labels, metrics, notes, and abstractions are representations, not reality itself. The principle is a warning to use models as tools while checking them against lived evidence, edge cases, and changing conditions.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/When to Trust Experts (and When Not To)
nodes.label: When to Trust Experts (and When Not To)
nodes.excerpt: Trusting experts is usually rational when the domain has real expertise, feedback loops, transparent methods, and consensus, but expert claims still need scope boundaries. Be more cautious when incentives are distorted, predictions are unfalsifiable, track records are hidden, or the expert is speaking outside their domain.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Changelog and Release Notes Writing
nodes.label: Changelog and Release Notes Writing
nodes.excerpt: Changelogs and release notes translate project changes into useful history for users, teammates, and future maintainers. Good entries group changes by impact, explain migration needs, and distinguish features, fixes, breaking changes, security updates, and known issues.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Code as Communication (Writing for Readers)
nodes.label: Code as Communication (Writing for Readers)
nodes.excerpt: Code is communication with future readers as much as instruction for machines. Good code makes intent, structure, constraints, and failure behavior legible so maintainers can change it without reconstructing the author's entire mental state.

nodes.label: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
nodes.excerpt: Decision-making under uncertainty means choosing without complete information by separating known facts, assumptions, probabilities, stakes, reversibility, and feedback loops. Good decisions are judged by process quality and expected value, not only by whether the outcome happened to work.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Ask for Help Effectively (XY Problem)
nodes.label: How to Ask for Help Effectively (XY Problem)
nodes.excerpt: Asking for help effectively means sharing the real goal, current approach, observed behavior, constraints, and what has already been tried. This avoids the XY problem, where someone asks about a chosen solution instead of the underlying problem.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Do a Brag Document
nodes.label: How to Do a Brag Document
nodes.excerpt: A brag document is a running record of accomplishments, impact, feedback, responsibilities, metrics, and lessons. It helps with performance reviews, resumes, interviews, promotion packets, and personal confidence because evidence is captured before memory fades.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Give a Good Demo
nodes.label: How to Give a Good Demo
nodes.excerpt: A good demo makes value visible quickly by showing a real workflow, not by reciting implementation details. It needs a clear audience, tight setup, realistic data, a rehearsed path, backup plan, and explicit takeaway.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Onboard onto a New Codebase
nodes.label: How to Onboard onto a New Codebase
nodes.excerpt: Onboarding onto a new codebase requires building a map of the system before trying to change it: setup, tests, entry points, data flow, ownership boundaries, conventions, and deployment path. The fastest path is to make small verified changes while documenting discoveries.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Run a Design Review
nodes.label: How to Run a Design Review
nodes.excerpt: A design review is a structured critique of a proposed technical approach before implementation. Good reviews test assumptions, alternatives, interfaces, failure modes, operational risk, and maintainability without turning into taste debates.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Run a Good Retrospective
nodes.label: How to Run a Good Retrospective
nodes.excerpt: A good retrospective turns recent work into specific learning and action without becoming blame, venting, or vague process theater. It asks what happened, what helped, what hurt, what should change, and who owns the follow-up.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Say No Without Burning Bridges
nodes.label: How to Say No Without Burning Bridges
nodes.excerpt: Saying no well means protecting priorities while preserving respect, clarity, and future relationship. The best refusals are timely, direct, brief, grounded in constraints, and sometimes paired with a smaller alternative.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Write a Good Technical Spec
nodes.label: How to Write a Good Technical Spec
nodes.excerpt: A good technical spec clarifies the problem, goals, non-goals, proposed design, alternatives, tradeoffs, risks, rollout, and validation plan before implementation. Its purpose is to make decisions explicit enough that reviewers can improve the plan while changes are still cheap.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Negotiating Job Offers — Full Playbook
nodes.label: Negotiating Job Offers — Full Playbook
nodes.excerpt: Negotiating job offers means improving compensation, role fit, start date, location, title, learning opportunity, and risk while preserving the relationship. A strong negotiation uses market data, alternatives, clear priorities, gratitude, and written confirmation.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Reading Other People's Code
nodes.label: Reading Other People's Code
nodes.excerpt: Reading other people's code is a deliberate skill: start from behavior, trace execution paths, inspect tests, map data flow, identify invariants, and resist judging style before understanding constraints. The goal is to reconstruct intent accurately enough to change the code safely.

nodes.label: Risk Management Frameworks
